for a long time in american politics weve been trapped in a cycle of everescalating political polarization as measured by voting patterns in us congress two parties have pulled apart to distances weve never seen before as measured by consistent partisan positioning among voters split in electorate has reached a historic level of divisiveness but this about to end weve now hit peak polarization forces that have fueled widening gap between two political parties are now fueling fights within two political parties fights that will lead to new coalitions in american politics eventually realigning two parties a new era of american politics about to emerge tautological reason polarization has increased in american politics that over past four decades conflict in american politics has increasingly operated along a single dimension republican versus democrat a large number of issues that were once nonpartisan or nonideological have become partisan issues almost every policy has now been swept into maw of partisan jockeying leaving almost no space for crosspartisan cooperation political system relies to function in order for congressional polarization to persist both parties have to maintain tight enough discipline over their members and political agenda to ensure consistent party voting and in order for public polarization to persist parties have to maintain tight enough message discipline among their elites to ensure that their voters only hear one main message this breaking down republicans are now in open warfare between trump supporters and nevertrumpers democrats are far less divided but internal rifts between their establishment hillary clinton and insurgent bernie sanders wings are also real and likely lasting this conflict emerging issues of international trade questions of corporate especially wall street power and in growing anger over money in politics and corruption generally in short parties are increasingly divided a growing range of issues that pit their lesseducated lowerincome voters who feel left behind by current politicaleconomic system against their bettereducated higherincome voter who dont want to mess too much with status quo these conflicts are not going away anytime soon this moment culmination of four interconnected but ultimately unsustainable trends that have turbocharged polarization over last two decades close competition fueled partisan nastiness and increased demand for campaign money demand for campaign money made parties more dependent wealthy donors made them less responsive to their voters this lack of responsiveness provided plenty of evidence for corruption and felt sense that politics was broken fueled anger both parties attempted to channel this anger against other party to distract from their own failures and contradictions and win elections by rendering other party toxic this exacerbated sense that politics was broken and corrupt these trends created contradictions and now these contradictions have created openings ambitious candidates who could get past their parties campaign finance gatekeepers had a lot of angry and leftbehind voters eager for their message and this precisely what sanders and especially donald trump have accomplished now there no going back these are big claims so lets flesh this story out a little more this time with expanded detail story could go all way back to decisive election of when democrats became dominant party in american politics for a generation holding together a bigtent new deal coalition that included southern prosegregationists with northern urban progressives but it was an uneasy alliance that could only last as long as civil rights legislation was bottled up then in democrats decidedly became party of civil rights and as lyndon johnson allegedly acknowledged upon signing civil rights act democrats have lost south for a generation democrats had controlled south ever since republicanled reconstruction since republicans were party of lincoln and of reconstruction but as republicans came to be party better aligned with south issues of race conservative republicans replaced conservative democrats in southern house and senate seats starting in s by when republicans won house for first time in years this transition was mostly complete by it was absolute and total as this all happened ideological center of republican party moved to south fusing social and economic conservatism northern liberal republicans were marginalized and soon endangered democrats meanwhile lost their southern conservative wing and ideological center of democratic party moved to coasts and big cities fusing social and economic liberalism as parties became less internally diverse individual members of congress delegated more power to their party leaders after all they all now basically agreed issues and they wanted leaders who could punish disloyal dissenters and control agenda so when newt gingrich took over speakership in he centralized power in position in a way it had not been centralized since in s american politics entered a somewhat unusual period of remarkably close twoparty competition for control of house and senate this as political scientist frances lee explains has been catalyst for a very nasty brand of partisan fighting this seems exactly right to and theres lots of evidence to prove it but not only has this close competition fueled partisanship by turning legislating into zerosum trench warfare it has also turbocharged fundraising dimension of political campaigning parties are campaigning harder than ever to win over those swing seats and this has meant raising everexpanding sums of money and in order to raise this money both parties have had to lean more and more their wealthiest donors but relying these wealthy donors created a problem for both parties many issues particularly economic issues wealthy elites hold separate opinions from most voters major republican donors generally want fiscal austerity and particularly a rolledback welfare state they also tend to be much more proimmigration and pro free trade than republican voters and not particularly worried about social issues but schemes like privatizing social security and voucherizing medicare have never been all that popular with actual republican voters and as middle classes wages have stagnated especially for those without college degrees and share of foreignborn residents in us has reached levels not seen since s it hit percent in voting constituency for antiimmigration populism has grown considerably democratic donors are somewhat more economically liberal but they are not about to support sandersstyle socialism they prefer clintons generally promarket views they will tolerate some regulation of business but not that much particularly when its tech and new economy businesses that they run and invest in whereas democrats once relied labor unions to get out vote by s unions could no longer provide support democrats needed democrats instead moved to depend professional class deprioritizing workers concerns to focus instead social and environmental concerns that went over much better in hollywood and san francisco and manhattan fundraisers for a while both parties could manage these contradictions being responsive to their donors while poohpoohing economic concerns of their less affluent voters bland promise that a thriving economy was good for everyone and for much of s and s economy was doing okay generally kept voters from feeling too angry and to extent that individual voters werent benefiting it was of course other partys fault as long as both sides were focused evils of other side and economy was not in a major recession party leaders could get away with ignoring many of their voters and using campaign contribution proceeds to make their case through more and more negative political advertising and aggressive media messaging this negativity translated into what political scientists alan abramowitz and steven webster call negative partisanship as they explain all these interrelated trends have turbocharged polarization over last two decades but they relied both sides being able to control anger that they were stoking and both sides being able to convince their voters that all of corruption and fecklessness in washington was because of other party this could not go indefinitely in fall financial crisis hit and government bailed out big wall street banks in a very public way for many this served as decisive proof that things really were rigged washington and wall street were in a corrupt alliance a conspiracy of career politicians and crony capitalists and lobbyists who were rolling in money and laughing about it while everyone else was living paycheck to paycheck as economy stumbled through recession and then a jobless recovery economic insecurity and political resentment increased obama and democrats swept election strength of antibush feeling and timeless energy of hope and change for first time since democrats had unified control in washington republicans were now out in cold with their backs against wall and democrats as new washington establishment republicans now turned their antigovernment rhetoric up to obama was stalin obama was hitler obama was a kenyanborn muslim bent destroying america democrats responded to charges with signature biggovernment legislation that taxed middle class so that poor people could have governmentsubsidized health care republican base went crazy all their worst fears were confirmed in tea party emerged representing what felt like new antiestablishment radicalism but was really just culmination of decades of republican antigovernment rhetoric now freed from any institutional responsibility for actually governing in strength of tea party antiobama energy and fact that democrats had won a bunch of majority republican house districts in and republicans swept back into control of house in election they finally won back senate but then nothing happened obamacare devil piñata of every republican attack was neither repealed nor replaced worse republican leaders were negotiating with obama satan himself they were letting obama get away with an executive order immigration here was most corrupt most crony capitalist administration in history and what were republicans in congress doing they were rolling over and being just as corrupt in june donald trump announced he was running for president and became immediate frontrunner strength of his aggressive antiimmigration stance because he had his own money and his own media celebrity trump did not need to do proausterity proimmigration profree trade dance that other potential frontrunners had done to shake big donor gop money tree he could just run for president declaring everything was corrupt and he was only one could trust because he was only one who didnt have a super pac and he could speak to workingclass republican voters who had been left behind in this economy by saying hed go after china and give them social security and medicare and go after corrupt hedge fund ripoff artists and they loved it for decades they had been told for partisan reasons to be angry they had been told for partisan reasons that washington was corrupt and that all washington politicians were evil now they finally had somebody who could say those things while actually not embodying any telltale signs of sins they also had somebody who could finally and authentically call out all corrupt things republican establishment types themselves were doing a few months later in september republican speaker john boehner announced he would resign from congress responding to efforts by house freedom caucus to force him out this was first time since that an insurgent faction in house had successfully challenged a sitting speaker antiestablishment anger that republicans had courted had now finally turned its leaders democratic side anticlinton progressives were hoping to draft elizabeth warren who had demonstrated her antiestablishment bona fides in december sinking obamas appointment of wall street banker antonio weiss for a top treasury position weiss withdrew his nomination instead accepting a counselor position to secretary jack lew warren had also been a prominent opponent of obamas major asian free trade agreement but warren didnt run instead it was selfidentified socialist bernie sanders who found opening democratic donor gatekeepers had cleared field for hillary this meant sanders could get attention just for being only real alternative attention that he was able to snowball into a following sanders wont win nomination but he has done far far better than anybody ever expected because a sizable number of democrat voters share his view that politics a rigged game where billionaires and crony capitalists always win and like sanders they are sick and tired of it if briefly scroll back to top of this article and look graph of polarization over time youll see a previous peak around or so while historical analogies are never perfect there are some notable similarities between now and around for one was last time a sitting speaker of us house had been effectively challenged from within party second in republican party was so divided over its presidential nomination that party splintered with about half of republicans supporting howard taft incumbent and about half supporting teddy roosevelt previous incumbent democrat woodrow wilson won in a landslide around was also when last great antiestablishment movement in america progressive movement emerged in response to growing concentrations of wealth and political power concentrations that many americans felt had left them behind as political scientist grant mcconnell once wrote of progressive legacy it consisted of a charges made against virtually all institutions of american society with one common theme corruption corruption of such prevalence disorder of such magnitude could only be explained by something more than assumption of a slowspreading decay theory of conspiracy was ready hand and in one way or another it was invoked as an explanation this resonates with todays antiestablishment mood political scientist hans noel has argued that emergence of progressive movement crosscut parties and eventually reshaped them noel notes that progressives opposed existing authority structures both economically eg trusts and politically they disliked political parties and other authority structures in it was progressive republican george norris who led internal house revolt against speaker joe cannon stripping cannon of most of his authority and devolving considerable powers back to individual members who had increasingly chafed under their marginalization like john boehner in cannon in represented culmination of exactly years of increasingly centralized leadership control in house speakership just as gingrich had radically centralized control in speaker thomas reed had radically centralized control in parties depolarized in s and s because freed of centralized leadership structures more legislating happened in committees where a crosscutting progressive coalition could more freely operate independently of two parties interestingly trade policy also became much less polarized in s with crossparty coalitions tariff issues most likely trump will be republican nominee even if nevertrump forces somehow wrest nomination from him unlikely but possible antiestablishment forces in republican party are not going away if not trump though guess he will stick around for a while longer somebody in tea party or possibly even ted cruz will find a way to harness trump voters by following trump issues playbook where there are voters to be had there are politicians to have them meanwhile in congress house speaker paul ryan already having difficulty building consensus around a budget process no matter how many speeches he gives about importance of decorum in politics it seems increasingly unlikely that he can reconcile conflicts that boehner failed to resolve means he will have to eventually lean democrats to pass a budget and like boehner before him alienate some of his party senate majority leader mitch mcconnell much less beloved even within his party will face similar problems most likely hillary clinton will become th us president she has led in every single headtohead poll against trump and most likely she will use her agendasetting powers to try to force republicans into open civil war by pushing many of issues that already divide them especially immigration and trade clintons natural home in probusiness center a position that will be advantageous to her and democrats in short term least but she has to be cautious emboldened by sanders and by elizabeth warren progressive wing of democrats growing and will be unhappy with clintons probusiness instincts internal fights will continue in both parties competing wings of both parties will feel that they are true republicansdemocrats growing importance of outside nonparty groups in elections will also force ideological diversity onto parties party leaders might instinctually want to wrest power back from these outside groups but theyd be wiser to open up their tent to allow for different ideas after all party that does best in american politics always one that can build broadest coalition means accepting ideological diversity eventually congressional leaders will realize or be forced to realize that only leadership style that works a less centralized more committeedriven approach this only way ideologically heterogeneous parties can effectively govern a more decentralized congress with more fluid coalitions will function better assuming that a more committeedriven process also accompanied by increases in congressional staffing capacity partisan control of congress will mean less since there will be more crossparty coalitions many issues like gun rights or affirmative action will remain very partisan but other issues especially those of corporatewall street power antitrust interventionist foreign policy will likely split parties trump republicans and sanders democrats will find common cause against establishment centrists big organized interests like chamber of commerce and other corporate groups will align less closely with republicans realizing that their future success will require right mix of republicans and democrats to advance their agendas and as parties become more ideological diverse voters who are generally more ideologically all over place than current party alignment would suggest will identify less reliably with one or other since there will be more for them in both parties they will again sometimes split their tickets depending who running many will feel more passionate about individual issues and will align themselves with supporters of those issues in both parties especially as individual interest groups become crosspartisan in order to achieve policy outcomes in that respect politics will come to look more like it did in s and s when liberal republicans existed alongside conservative democrats this an optimistic scenario but it only works if party leaders tolerate diversity within their party and allow disagreements another scenario that establishment republicans banish trump faction and democrats banish sanders faction after elections and both parties go back to predictable and intractable trench warfare battle lines that have become increasingly dug in over past two decades using nastier and nastier tactics to subvert internal divisions in service of larger fight against a common enemy this may be possible for a little while longer especially if economy improves significantly but it still probably longterm unsustainable for reasons ive described above it also may mean that becomes an even more violent and nasty election another possibility that parties realign quickly with trumptea party faction effecting a rapid transformation of republican party into a downscale nationalist populist party pushing remaining upperclass moderate republicans into a more probusiness democratic party in turn pushes some disaffected sanders voters into republican party if this realignment happens too quickly there no period of depolarization but this seems unlikely given stickiness of partisan identity and strong disagreements between two parties a whole range of other issues other scenarios are possible as well especially if there are significant global crises but heres bottom line something different this year in american politics logic that has operated for past two decades or so breaking down largely because factors and trends that propelled it produced unsustainable contradictions american politics now entering a new logic with new trends and forces that will push lines of political conflict in directions are only beginning to understand this feels like chaos and it but it also good news because chaos scrambles rules weve hit peak polarization politics slowly coming unstuck a period of new possibilities awaits this post part of polyarchy an independent blog produced by political reform program new america a washington think tank devoted to developing new ideas and new voices see more polyarchy posts here

and personal emails during her four years as secretary of state after that judicial watch foia case was reinstated and then judge in case demanded of state that it produce clinton s emails when judicial watch expressed frustration to judge about pace it was getting emails judge ordered clinton under penalty of perjury to certify that she had surrendered all her governmental emails to state clean her server she acknowledged that she decided emails were personal and were selected as governmental and returned governmental ones to state department she has denied steadfastly and consistently that she ever sent or received any materials marked classified while secretary of state using her private server all of her behavior has triggered fbi investigation because she may have committed workrelated emails of a government employee belong to government she stole that data metadata do not appear her paper copies hence argument that she stole and destroyed governmentowned metadata this particularly troublesome for her present political ambitions because of a federal statute that disqualifies from public office all who have stolen federal property she probably already barred from public office though this was not prominently raised when she entered us senate or department of state because of china silverware and furniture that she and her husband took from white house in january clinton may also have committed espionage by failing to secure government secrets entrusted to her she did that by diverting those secrets to an unprotected nongovernmental venue her own server and again by emailing those secrets to other unprotected and nongovernmental venues reason she can deny sending or receiving anything marked classified that protected government secrets are not marked classified so her statement though technically true highly misleading governmental designations of protected secrets are confidential secret and top secret not classified state department investigators have found emails sent or received by clinton in least one of those three categories of protected secrets back when clinton became secretary of state her first day in office she had an hourlong fbi briefing proper and lawfully required care of government secrets she signed a statement under penalty of perjury acknowledging that she knew law and that it content of emails not any stamped markings that makes them secret earlier this week fox news colleagues confirmed certain presence of topsecret materials among emails intelligence from foreign sources or about foreign hand nearly equaled by number of journalists death watch before rubio arrived a prankster hijacked microphone and was chased off by campaign aides when rubio himself spoke sound system failed so he delivered his valedictory with a bullhorn gop establishment has failed it s up to voters to deny trump rubio s voice sounded tinny but his words were rich with nostalgia as he recalled knocking doors when he ran for city commissioner here in between sips of sweet cuban coffee heard stories of their youth of dreams they lost he said that has carried every single day throughout this campaign knowing that worst days are better than some of best days that many people in this community have had these are not best days for rubio or for anybody who cares about american democracy yearold made mistakes during his campaign freezing in new hampshire debate failing to take trump earlier then finally attacking trump by joking about genitalia yet he finished honorably he spoke reflectively monday about s dangerous it leads he said to where are today a nation where people literally hate each other because they re voting for different candidates and it leaves us incapable of solving problems trump tore up norms of decency that remained in american politics and rubio expressed puzzlement that it worked whole life ve been told being humble a virtue and now being humble a weakness and being vain and selfabsorbed somehow a virtue he said whole life ve been told no matter how feel about someone respect everyone because are all children of same god and now being respectful to one another considered political correctness rubio voiced regret for his own role in vulgarity saying he felt terrible for joking about trump s penis size such remorse separates rubio from trump who seems to have only this time it s directed gop that s welcoming country to rubio s parents immigrated settling among shoebox homes here in cuban west miami everywhere go tell story of this community of people many cleared senate judiciary committee in february still she waited longer than seven most recent us attorneys general combined for a vote senate floor after majority leader mitch mcconnell insisted first finishing work an unrelated bill loretta lynchs father lorenzo a lynch was in senate gallery watching when historic vote took place confirming her daughter as first african american female attorney general good guys won thats what has happened in this country all along lorenzo lynch told reporters even during slavery levi coffin was a founder of underground railroad even during slavery a white man fought against slavery so all over this land good folks have stood in right lane in right path a twotime us attorney for eastern district of new york lynch takes highprofile job time when america faces a series of challenges from dealing with strained relations and deep distrust in some cities between police and communities they serve to criminal justice reform to confronting ongoing threat of terrorism lynch has earned a reputation as a highly qualified but lowprofile prosecutor who has a good relationship with law enforcement and a history of handling tough cases well she a good listener and a skilled consensus builder qualities that will help her succeed justice said tim heaphy a former us attorney for western district of virginia who served under lynch attorney generals advisory committee a group that meets regularly to advise justice department policy matters in that attorney general job are center of so many of emerging significant pressing issues not only in this country but around world theres probably no job in government as diverse and challenging as being attorney general of united states heaphy said he added that building support for initiatives both within and outside department an important part of job she will be good getting people to work well together think thats a strength of hers saw that committee heaphy said lynchs portfolio will include addressing voting rights whitecollar crime and policy reviews as well as public corruption an area in she has vast experience in a statement obama said america will be better off with lynch leading department of jusice lorettas confirmation ensures that are better positioned to keep communities safe keep nation secure and ensure that every american experiences justice under law obama said in a statement shortly after vote lynchs experience civil rights case like helping win convictions of new york city police officers who sexually assaulted haitian immigrant abner louima will be important as her office tackles closely watched investigations in recent police conduct cases including still unexplained death of a yearold baltimore man while in police custody shes seen and understands injustices that have taken place in past and so therefore shes uniquely also equipped to deal with whats going and do kinds of investigations that will restore faith to americans in their justice system said rep greg meeks dnew york born in greensboro north carolina lynch grew up miles to east in durham north carolina her father was a fourthgeneration baptist minister her mother an english teacher and school gop opposes kind of antitrust regulation that would preserve freedom of expression online america s right wing in a froth about allegations that facebook has tweaked its trending news feed to reduce visibility of conservative news sites it s not clear if allegations from a gizmodo report based anonymous sources are true and facebook denies them but deeper problem undeniably real facebook dominant member of a small number of giant entities corporate and governmental that are gaining control over news freedom of expression and much of digital lives irony that conservatives and businessbacked republicans in congress who howl about facebook are same people who have thwarted policies that would encourage competition need to challenge that increasingly centralized control almost no one wants to address fact that facebook becoming a monopoly in antitrust sense of word along with google it dominates online advertising facebook especially does so mobile devices are way many people connect to internet if offer news and information online have almost no choice but to play facebook s field because so much of audience there in some parts of world facebook essentially internet because mobile devices are pretty much sole means of online access and in some cases company has made deals with local telecommunications companies and or governments facebook has been buying everything that presents even a whiff of competition instagram whatsapp oculus this smart no one can dispute that mark zuckerberg and others his team are brilliant technologists and strategists but it s also a red flag as zuckerberg famously said several years ago he wants facebook to be like electricity well electricity a utility and regulate utilities monopolies and cozy oligopolies never turn out well in long run for anyone but monopolists or cartel members they end up controlling markets and do their best to thwart genuine competition it s their nature why capitalism plainly best system when it s working right needs rules to promote competition yet republicans in general think government should play little to no role in promoting competition they consider antitrust inquiry and enforcement to be counterproductive best except of course when a powerful constituent a corporation usually in danger from predatory behavior that attitude accounts for gop s cheerleading for corporate dominance of internet access republicans in general are fine with idea that one or two companies say leading cable provider and another telecom should control access in most communities and utterly opposed to a remedy what call network neutrality to ensure that people edges of networks not dominant internet service providers should decide what information they want and what priority don t want government to tell facebook what it can publish and don t look forward to much more than posturing from congress but do want government to start paying extremely close attention to way company becoming a monopoly and to what it means for freedom of expression when a single company has so much power over what people say online want government to use antitrust and other procompetition laws to ensure that facebook doesn t abuse its dominance in a business sense want governments to promote open technology and communications and fierce competition every level kudos to zuckerberg for making facebook so appealing to millions of users that s an amazing achievement but can t allow facebook to leverage that success to block emergence of alternatives to its service or use its market power to influence or alter content of publications and others trying to communicate with facebook users all need to wake up to potential threat facebook poses to freedom of expression once are in its enclosed online space it corporation s terms of service not first amendment that determines what can say if it decides to downplay speech it doesn t like facebook has right to do so so m glad that conservatives are concerned even if allegations prove overblown facebook has modified its outright denial to a re looking into it stance stay tuned d be even happier if conservatives realized that government does have a role in promoting genuine competition and that re in uncharted informationfreedom territory under new control freaks of silicon valley dan gillmor teaches digital media literacy arizona state university he author of mediactive he wrote this for zocalo public square in addition to its own editorials usa today publishes diverse opinions from outside writers including board of contributors to last texas republican to occupy oval office george w bush took percent of state s hispanic vote in his presidential reelection setting a relatively high bar for handful of texasborn or raised republicans who might be hoping to follow in his footsteps in republican presidential aspirants with ties to lone star state must figure out how to hold gop base and attract conservative hispanics if they want to be successful in texas political observers say so how do partys four most prominent texas affiliated mightbe candidates former gov rick perry jeb bush son and brother of two former presidents from state texas junior us senator ted cruz and us sen rand paul of kentucky stack up in early going for republicans to avoid a repeat of when presidential nominee mitt romney took only percent of hispanic vote nationwide they need to nominate a conservative candidate who can go into hispanic communities and truly connect with voters said hector de leon cochairman of associated republicans of texas reaches out to hispanic voters it s all about paying attention de leon said hispanics made up percent of national electorate in presidential election but in texas they make up almost onethird of eligible voters and there s plenty room for improvement when it comes to voter turnout only percent of texas hispanics eligible to vote cast ballots in political observers say candidates would be right to take a page out of george w bush s playbook hispanic outreach he solidified his winning record with hispanics with help from latinomedia guru lionel sosa who told texas tribune he has been in talks with jeb bush about his possible presidential bid sosa helped george w bush s campaign craft several television ads that painted him as candidate who understood hispanic culture candidate who can mobilize onthefence hispanic voters who usually do not turn out to vote could win state do think primaries will include a concerted effort by some candidates to speak to that constituency said sylvia manzano a senior analyst for nonpartisan political polling organization latino decisions in texas that s million people that s a number that cannot be ignored though its still early in game many political observers say jeb bush best positioned moment he grew up in midland spent much of his childhood in houston and considered friendly to hispanic community both personally and politically jeb bush not going to come in and play mariachi politics manzano said he knows better than that but this far out all speculation as candidates tiptoe toward starting gate heres how several political experts handicap field already holding a political advantage because he fluent in spanish former florida governor has experience winning over hispanics in a state where they make up a large part of population during his reelection campaign bush won an impressive percent of floridas hispanic electorate its worth noting though that florida s mostly cuban hispanic population differs from texas where a majority of hispanics have roots in mexico what experts say for bush reaching out to texas hispanics would be an extended family affair aside from benefiting from groundwork his family has done in state expect to see bush campaigning with his mexicanborn wife columba and his son texas land commissionergeorge p bush his side george p also fluent in spanish and helped found hispanic republicans of texas a political group that recruits and supports hispanic republicans running for public office bush s record issues that resonate with texas hispanics particularly immigration reform could prove attractive to this voting group he has urged congress to pass immigration reform and has highlighted it as a key issue in helping republicans win hispanics he also gained national attention last year when he said many of those entering country illegally do so out of an act of love for their families as state s longestserving governor perry has long courted texas hispanics he has steadily improved his standing since winning only percent of hispanic vote when he defeated hispanic businessman tony sanchez of laredo in by time he was reelected in perry pulled in percent of hispanic voters what experts say perry s efforts to broaden his appeal were buoyed by passage of texas dream act during his year tenure though future of law granting instate tuition to some undocumented immigrants unclear perry has stood by it both national stage and home during a presidential debate perry famously told opponents who challenged his support of law that they had no heart more recently as state s new gop leadership works to overturn law perry has been vocal about his continued support for it because he presided over state s economic boom in last decade perry has a unique opportunity to appeal to hispanics economic issues if perry can convince hispanic voters that they benefited from socalled texas miracle he may be able to sway some onthefence voters his way though he only hispanic in group and first hispanic senator from texas cruz has largely avoided making heritage part of his political persona beyond recounting his father s journey to united states from cuban as an exile in still he has done well with texas hispanics in he outperformed republican presidential nominee mitt romney taking percent of hispanic vote according to a latino decisions poll what experts say cruz arguably faces toughest challenge courting texas hispanics given his divisive tone immigration and health care he has been vocal in his opposition to president obama s executive order immigration will grant millions of undocumented immigrants work permits and reprieve from deportation proceedings order widely popular among hispanics health care cruz has been one of biggest foes of federal affordable care act texas hispanics who make up a large portion of state s uninsured population overwhelmingly support health law though paul was elected to senate from kentucky where hispanics make up only percent of population he grew up in lake jackson texas where hispanics are onefifth of population paul has spent last few months preaching a message of hispanic inclusion within republican ranks what experts say paul someone to watch in upcoming election when it comes to appealing to texas hispanics because of his views growing gop s number of hispanic supporters because he largely unknown among texas hispanics paul also has some room to improve his standing a november poll by latino decisions found that almost a third of texas latino voters have no opinion of paul

fight for republican presidential nomination appears to be moving into a new more fluid phase no longer question merely whether or how donald trump can be stopped recent rise in polls of retired neurosurgeon ben carson trump s lowkey stylistic opposite has shown that celebrity billionaire may not be only one who can tap appetite of many in party s angry base for an outsider and after wednesday s chaotic and freewheeling debate there also a new dynamic establishment side of race former florida governor jeb bush s onceformidable campaign appears to be nearing a state of collapse made worse by his flailing stage in colorado that has provided an opening to his onetime ally sen marco rubio of florida who getting a new look from party establishment an ironic situation given rubio s roots as an insurgent tea party favorite in marco rubio now has probably best shot to emerge as mainstream alternative to trump and carson said ari fleischer who was press secretary for president george w bush more broadly fleischer who not committed to any of candidates predicted that gop about to enter a condensed version of where it was four years ago where party volatile and shopping around that could help ted cruz who also made a strong showing in debate firebrand senator from texas widely despised by washington republican hierarchy looking to nudge out trump and carson among voters who are looking for a candidate to supplant old order don t think party going to nominate anybody who has not been elected before said stuart stevens who was a top strategist for gop nominee mitt romney also likely to force some clarity in coming weeks calendar first contest in iowa barely more than three months away so focus for all leading contenders will have to shift from raising their profiles nationally to refining strategies and organization it will take to put specific states in their column campaign really in a nutsandbolts stage said trump s campaign manager corey lewandowski it s about getting ballot organizing and making sure people understand what caucuses are in iowa and other states and teach them how to participate it s about getting people committed and ready lewandowski said trump s campaign already hiring staff for states that do not hold their primaries until march approach to execute and meet criteria to get ballot in all states in five territories and in district of columbia that s a fulltime job he added this campaign doing all of things necessary for longterm success same time trump seemed to be moderating and refining his message as he campaigned outside reno nev thursday afternoon where he previously has devoted his rallies to slinging insults his opponents and boasting about his poll numbers trump focused instead describing professional and life experience he would bring to white house he also cited issues where he claimed to have led before it was a popular thing to do including opposing iraq war and aggressively combating illegal immigration that s kind of thinking need in country trump said a lot of people in audience maybe in small way have that same thinking bush campaigning in new hampshire insisted that his struggling candidacy should not be counted out former florida governor who polling in single digits almost everywhere insisted re doing fine but he appeared to acknowledge that he had not helped his prospects with his showing in debate look there are two types of politicians there are talkers and there are doers bush told crowd wish could talk as well as some of people stage big personalities stage but m a doer rubio meanwhile must capi tal ize quickly whatever interest and momentum may be generated by his debate performance he spent morning making rounds of six network and cable television shows and remainder of day fundraising in denver and chicago he will be in iowa friday cbs this morning rubio declined to criticize bush personally and said differences between two will be fleshed out in terms of policy m going to continue to tell people who am what m for there are policy differences between us ll discuss those americans deserve to hear those but m not going to change campaign he said jeb friend have admiration for him m not running against him m running for president cruz meanwhile building what gop insiders say a strong organization campaign says that it has volunteers ground with in first four voting states fundraising has also been robust and was reignited by debate during senator trained his fire cnbc moderators and media a cruz aide said more than million had poured in since wednesday night s debate there will be growing pressure candidates who are getting no traction to get out of race thursday for instance new york times editorial board called upon new jersey gov chris christie to abandon his bid new jersey in trouble and governor off pursuing a presidential run that s turned out to be nothing more than a vanity project paper wrote mr christie s numbers are in basement and he s nearly out of campaign cash this his moment all right to go home and use rest of his term to clean out barn as speaker john boehner would say christie for his part tweeted can t read article because don t have a subscription but can tell this am not going anywhere robert costa in colorado and jenna johnson in nevada contributed to this report

late evening of aug couldn t sleep was due to substituteanchor msnbc s up with steve kornacki and should have been asleep but after looking twitter feed and reading rage under ferguson felt compelled to type a reaction to killing of michael brown by police officer darren wilson tying shooting to inane whine of certain politicians about a war whites decried next morning death of yet another unarmed black man hands of a white police officer in those early hours and early days there was more unknown than known but this month justice department released two mustread investigations connected to killing of brown that filled in blanks corrected record and brought sunlight to dark places by revealing ugly practices that institutionalized racism and hardship they have also forced to deal with two uncomfortable truths brown never surrendered with his hands up and wilson was justified in shooting brown report ferguson police department detailed abuse and blatant trampling of constitutional rights of people mostly african americans in ferguson years of mistreatment by police courts and municipal government including evidence that all three balanced their books backs of people of ferguson were laid bare in damning pages overwhelming data from doj provided background and muchneeded context for why a small st louis suburb most had never heard of exploded moment brown was killed his death gave voice to many who suffered in silence unarmed yearold also became a potent symbol of lack of trust between african americans and law enforcement not just in ferguson but in rest of country lord knows there have been plenty of recent examples and militarized response to protesters by local police put an exclamation point demonstrators concerns but other doj report one actual shooting of michael brown shows him to be an inappropriate symbol through exhaustive interviews with witnesses crosschecking their statements with previous statements to authorities and media ballistics dna evidence and results from three autopsies justice department was able to present a credible and troubling picture of what happened canfield drive more credible than grand jury decision to not indict wilson transcript of his grand jury testimony read like so much handholding by prosecution what doj found made ill wilson knew about theft of cigarillos from convenience store and had a description of suspects brown fought with officer and tried to take his gun and popular handsup storyline isn t corroborated by ballistic and dna evidence and multiple witness statements was perpetuated by witness in fact just about everything said to media by witness whom all know as dorian johnson friend with brown that day was not supported by evidence and other witness statements page brown then grabbed weapon and struggled with wilson to gain control of it wilson fired striking brown in hand autopsy results and bullet trajectory skin from brown s palm outside of suv door as well as brown s dna inside of driver s door corroborate wilson s account that during struggle brown used his right hand to grab and attempt to control wilson s gun according to three autopsies brown sustained a close range gunshot wound to fleshy portion of his right hand base of his right thumb soot from muzzle of gun found embedded in tissue of this wound coupled with indicia of thermal change from heat of muzzle indicate that brown s hand was within inches of muzzle of wilson s gun when it was fired location of recovered bullet in side panel of driver s door just above wilson s lap also corroborates wilson s account of struggle over gun and when gun was fired as do witness accounts that wilson fired least one shot from inside suv page although there are several individuals who have stated that brown held his hands up in an unambiguous sign of surrender prior to wilson shooting him dead their accounts do not support a prosecution of wilson as detailed throughout this report some of those accounts are inaccurate because they are inconsistent with physical and forensic evidence some of those accounts are materially inconsistent with that witness s own prior statements with no explanation credible or otherwise as to why those accounts changed over time certain other witnesses who originally stated brown had his hands up in surrender recanted their original accounts admitting that they did not witness shooting or parts of it despite what they initially reported either to federal or local law enforcement or to media prosecutors did not rely those accounts when making a prosecutive decision while credible witnesses gave varying accounts of exactly what brown was doing with his hands as he moved toward wilson ie balling them holding them out or pulling up his pants up and varying accounts of how he was moving ie charging moving in slow motion or running they all establish that brown was moving toward wilson when wilson shot him although some witnesses state that brown held his hands up shoulder level with his palms facing outward for a brief moment these same witnesses describe brown then dropping his hands and charging wilson doj report notes page that johnson made multiple statements to media immediately following incident that spawned popular narrative that wilson shot brown executionstyle as he held up his hands in surrender in one of those interviews johnson told msnbc that brown was shot in back by wilson it was then that johnson said brown stopped turned around with his hands up and said don t have a gun stop shooting and like that hands up don t shoot became mantra of a movement but it was wrong built a lie yet this does not diminish importance of real issues unearthed in ferguson by brown s death nor does it discredit what has become larger black lives matter in fact false ferguson narrative stuck because of concern over a distressing pattern of other police killings of unarmed african american men and boys around time of brown s death eric garner was killed a staten island street july john crawford iii was killed in a walmart in beavercreek ohio aug four days before brown levar jones survived being shot by a south carolina state trooper sept tamir rice years old was killed in a cleveland park nov day before ferguson grand jury opted not to indict wilson sadly list has grown longer now that black lives matter to everyone it imperative that continue marching for and giving voice to those killed in racially charged incidents hands of police and others but must never allow to march under banner of a false narrative behalf of someone who would otherwise offend sense of right and wrong and when discover that have must acknowledge it admit error and keep marching that s what ve done here

democratic national committee officials saturday turned down bernie sanders formal request for ouster of aggressive attack surrogates for hillary clinton from key national convention committees campaign announced earlier that it wanted to remove connecticut gov dannel malloy as a cochairman of platform committee and former massachusetts rep barney frank as head of rules committee frank has sharply criticized sanders positions breaking up big banks and malloy has criticized sanders guns governor malloy and mr frank have both been aggressive attack surrogates for clinton campaign sanders campaign counsel brad deutsch wrote in a letter to party s rules and bylaws committee their criticisms of sen sanders have gone beyond dispassionate ideological disagreement and have exposed a deeper professional political and personal hostility toward senator and his campaign but committee cochairs jim roosevelt and lorraine miller responded that his challenge fails to meet criteria for their dismissal it did not allege any violations in conduct of their elections to their posts by dnc executive committee in january they wrote sanders campaign s friday letter to democratic national committee rules officials marks latest turn in sanders feud with party officials it comes a week after sanders said he would make sure rep debbie wasserman schultz of florida isn t reappointed to her role as head of dnc if he becomes president and he began fundraising for her primary opponent tim canova most members of convention standing committees rules platform and credentials are awarded to candidates based results of those primaries and caucuses but under party rules wasserman schultz recommended cochairs and a slate of atlarge appointments per committee for approval by party s executive committee in a may letter to wasserman schultz sanders complained she selected only three of his more than recommendations for committee appointments and he expressed no confidence in malloy or frank writing their appointments suggest committees are being established in an overtly partisan way deutsch argued friday that frank and malloy can t be relied upon to perform convention duties fairly while laboring under such deeply held bias appointment of two individuals so outspokenly critical of sen sanders and so closely affiliated with secretary clintons campaign raises concerns that two of three convention standing committees are being constituted in an overtly partisan way designed to exclude meaningful input from supporters of sen sanders candidacy deutsch wrote in april tad devine sanders senior adviser said frank among surrogates for hillary clinton who used sanders remarks to a new york daily news editorial board april to promote a story line that questions sanders capacity to be president devine pointed to frank s april statements msnbc that sanders confused several things in his responses to questions about his core issue of breaking up big banks frank also said sanders responses to editorial board were not coherent frank said in an april interview with usa today that he would step aside from his cochairmanship if democratic nomination still uncertain in june and if a rules committee decision could be deciding factor with clintons decisive lead in delegates that appears unlikely a connecticut democratic party spokesman told usa today earlier this month that malloy agrees with sanders many issues saturday leigh appleby said malloy committed to a platform and process reflective of partys diverse viewpoints this years democratic platform process washington president obama has quietly racked up a series of legislative victories during past few months as lawmakers have enthusiastically embraced his calls for a higher minimum wage paid sick leave and universal prekindergarten instead of capitol hill those victories happened in city halls state houses and county buildings far from washington least six major cities chicago philadelphia pittsburgh seattle tacoma wash and washington dc have passed paid sick leave laws in four months since obama called for state and local action in this years state of union address since address when obama called for an increase in minimum wage states and six major cities have taken action including los angeles last week obamas stateandlocal strategy may be unprecedented in its scope and ambition though previous administrations have appointed top advisers to listen to concerns of state and local officials obama white house appears to be first to aggressively use those same channels to encourage them to adopt obamas policies it a change in paradigm where used to sit passively by waiting for elected officials to come to us think can have a more substantial impact if collaborate said valerie jarrett assistant to president for public engagement and intergovernmental affairs think president has always had perspective that change always happens from ground up and state and local officials are oftentimes more influenced by will of american people than politics in washington would seem to indicate jarrett said in an interview obama has no formal authority over state and local lawmakers so his persuasion a form of soft power phone part of what obama has described as a penandphone strategy to take action in absence of congressional cooperation hes found fertile ground in democraticrun cities such as seattle where mayor ed murray helped push for a minimum wage and paid leave laws that have gone into effect since april president obama recognizes that good ideas are being incubated local level and those are ideas that are going to go to scale nationally murray said that recognition can go a long way in a city such as seattle often doesnt get national attention east coast cities do president of united states recognizing us only helps us do other things beside ecommerce and coffee murray said theres also resistance jon russell a councilman in culpeper va and director of american city county exchange a yearold initiative of smallgovernment american legislative exchange council russell said states not cities not congress and not president should be primary regulator of labor conditions obama he said hopskipping over states with number of states that have now turned to opposite political party he doesnt have allies there that he used to russell said to work with urban areas to push his agenda not surprising its only allies he really has strategy has been more effective some policies than others since last september more than mayors have signed to brothers keeper initiative a commitment to help boys and young men of color but since obama called for states to offer free community college in january only a handful have moved in that direction stateandlocal strategy effort has been particularly effective paid leave policies obama championed in his state of union address this year fortythree million workers have no paid sick leave million think about that obama said so ill be taking new action to help states adopt paid leave laws of their own that pledge came with a million budget from department of labor to help fund feasibility studies for state and local governments will begin to be awarded this summer obama dispatched jarrett and labor secretary tom perez a lead leave tour of cities that have adopted paid leave policies jarrett went to philadelphia and chicago perez has been to portland ore pawtucket ri st petersburg fla and seattle tuesday perez will be in minneapolis just adopted a paid leave policy for city employees this month perez said its a mistake to view stateandlocal strategy as separate from a larger effort strategy an alloftheaboveandthensome strategy he said he said obama will continue to use executive orders as he did when he increased minimum wage for federal contractors to an hour want congress to act and im confident that its a when question not an if question but were also not going to wait around for congress to act perez said although focusing city councils may seem small compared with sweeping congressional legislation early in obamas presidency state minimum wage laws will raise pay of more than million people by that number comes from white house council of economic advisers includes people making just above minimum wage who were helped indirectly because their wages are increased accordingly thats nothing to sneeze thats progress jarrett said san francisco passed first paid sick time law in nation in requiring all employers to grant one hour of paid sick time for every hours worked three states and cities now have paid sick leave laws books according to a better balance a new yorkbased legal advocacy group that tracks such laws a year ago advocates had a hard time breaking through public consciousness issue said groups copresident sherry leiwant after a white house working families summit last year and again after januarys state of union address theres been an explosion of interest by state and local lawmakers she said white house leadership this issue has been huge its had a real impact leiwant said that influence mayors or governors who are democrats but havent paid a lot of attention to this issue or they want to be probusiness democrats this gives them a lot of cover a better balance still favors a national law that would cover everyone but while government divided national level best strategy to go to localities and states a citybycity strategy can be painstaking and legally complicated every state grants different home rule powers to cities and even different powers to differentsized cities in same state philadelphia was able to pass a paid leave bill applying to privatesector workers but pittsburghs law applies only to some nonunion city workers pittsburgh city councilwoman natalia rudiak credits obama for giving her citys effort a lot of momentum but she said more cities need to put pressure state legislature to adopt statewide law theres also pushback from opposite direction last month pennsylvania senate voted to preempt philadelphia law taking power to regulate sick leave out of hands of local governments that bills sponsor state sen john eichelberger jr said its untenable to have municipalities with different labor standards forcing businesses to comply with a patchwork of rules philosophically have a real problem with president obama going to a municipality and trying to accomplish an agenda whether its liberal or conservative or otherwise he said its just not place to adopt these policies obama has opened an entirely new frontier of presidential power by turning to state and local governments said matthew eshbaughsoha who studies effect of presidential persuasion university of north texas im really struck by one why hasnt anybody thought of this before and two this could be a very effective strategy eshbaughsoha said a time when executive orders are becoming particularly controversial and youre not able to break through gridlock of congress think its ingenious hes skeptical that effort will put much pressure members of congress who dont support obamas policies jarrett said effort starting to snowball she said some city leaders agreed with paid leave principle but were reluctant to pass what could be seen as burdensome regulations in a difficult economy as more cities and states have passed those policies its emboldened others success begets success she said what evidence has begun to show its not a burden but an investment that starting to pay off indeed philadelphia mayor michael nutter vetoed two paid leave bills in and but three weeks after obamas state of union address this year nutter reversed course what changed nutter said economy was better and bill contained key compromises to accommodate small businesses nutter spokesman mark mcdonald said he was unaware of any role obama had in getting bill passed bill greenlee councilman who sponsored measure said nutter completely turned a corner paid leave when president put it his agenda gotta think that when president mentions sick leave in his state of union and if youre a democrat and a supporter of presidents agenda in general thats gotta have an effect greenlee said nutter did make a symbolic nod to obamas influence in signing paid leave bill he signed it with a pen obama gave him after president used it to sign an unemployment compensation bill only other time nutter used that pen was an executive order raising minimum wage for city contractors to an hour

with shake of an etchasketch mitt romney reintroduced himself to republican party friday as a man interested in running for president because of his desire to address poverty and income inequality one only wonders why former governor of massachusetts neglected to focus growing problems last time he held title of gop standard bearer addressing a gathering of republican national committee officials below deck of decommissioned uss midway aircraft carrier in san diego california romney ticked off three priorities crucial to what he called postobama era making world safer with a more muscular foreign policy providing opportunity to all americans and lifting people out of poverty its a tragedy a human tragedy that middle class in this country by and large doesnt believe that future will be better than past he said havent seen rising incomes over decades rich have gotten richer income inequality has gotten worse and there are more people in poverty than ever before under this president he added romney stressed his years as an lds pastor a topic he and his campaign rarely broached in and described working with people who are very poor to help them get help governor isnt only potential republican presidential candidate to embrace a more populist tone as wage growth continues to lag in an accelerating economic recovery former florida gov jeb bush recently lamented that while last eight years have been pretty good ones for topearners theyve been a lost decade for rest of america former pennsylvania sen rick santorum has emphasized reconnecting with blue collar americans even diehard conservatives like sens rand paul rky and ted cruz rtexas paid lip service to matter heritage foundation policy summit an annual gathering of conservatives in washington while he may be sincere in his pursuit to eliminate poverty notion that romney would be best candidate to lead his party in doing so puzzling best former massachusetts governor didnt have just one misstep that allowed democrats and republican primary opponents to paint him as an obscenely wealthy outoftouch plutocrat in election he had a dozen heres just a brief sampling im in this race because care about americans im not concerned about very poor have a safety net there if it needs repair ill fix it he said inelegantly following his victory in florida primary im not concerned about very rich theyre doing just fine im concerned about very heart of america percent percent of americans who right now are struggling corporations are people friend of course they are everything corporations earn ultimately goes to people he said before iowa ames straw poll and his infamous remarks about percent of americans job not to worry about those people ill never convince them that they should take personal responsibility and care for their lives but its not just gaffes romney embraced paul ryan budget in a sweeping plan that if enacted would have instituted draconian cuts to programs affecting poor millions of lowincome americans benefiting from medicare medicaid and social security as well as other retirement funding would have been affected under a romney presidency added to his promise to repeal affordable care act and its subsidies for poor its not hard to see how romney wouldnt be vexed by some of same problems should he ultimately decide to run for president
