m not sure when it started but some point republican party ceded business of governance to democrats maybe it began with tea party movement or fox news or larger conservative mediaindustrial complex honestly don t know but it s clear now that gop no longer a legitimate governing party a party that allows rank neophytes like herman cain and donald trump and ben carson and carly fiorina to run for highest office in country has lost its way if look how republican party operates today it s hard to avoid conclusion that governing just isn t a priority or internal pressures within party make it impossible instead gop has become what previously called a selfperpetuating hype machine for conservative political entrepreneurs particularly national level republican candidates and legislators many of them least show no interest in compromise or serious policymaking what d expect from a party of and for purists while new gop has been bad for country it s been great for political celebrities people looking to promote their personal brands ted cruz most recent and obvious example of this approach to politics cruz has been a remarkably ineffective senator he has done nothing but bloviate and showboat senate floor he s accomplished zero legislatively his only practical contribution has been to obstruct and draw attention to his martyrdom read presidential campaign ted cruz will never be elected president if he manages to win republican nomination he ll lose in a landslide to a democrat whoever that happens to be and because he s so eagerly made a spectacle of himself in senate he s alienated all but tiniest segment of his own party means he has no political capital in congress hardly a concern for someone uninterested in legislating however cruz s latest squabble with rand paul helps to illustrate cruz s intentions in an interview with fox news brian kilmeade paul basically wrote cruz s political obituary ted has chosen to make this really personal and chosen to call people dishonest in leadership and call them names really goes against decorum and also against rules of senate and as a consequence he can t get anything done legislatively he pretty much done for and stifled and it s really because of personal relationships or lack of personal relationships and it a problem approach things a little different am still just as hardcore in saying what are doing just chose not to call people liars senate floor and it s just a matter of different perspectives how best to get to end result paul right of course but he omits an essential point cruz has been ineffective by design managing relationships and respecting decorum only matter to people trying to accomplish things in senate that s not what cruz up to like fanatical tea party wing of house cruz there to obstruct and selfpromote in all likelihood cruz will retire after a single term in senate now that he s boosted his national profile and endeared himself to insurgent elements of base he can pivot to private sector and make more money as a professional conservative activist as for example jim demint did in cruz ought to be seen as grifter that he it was never about policy for him when he leaves senate he ll be a hero to fringe right he ll make a fortune conservative lecture circuit telling rapturous audiences about his willingness to challenge washington cartel it won t matter that he accomplished nothing changed nothing it ll be enough that he pretended to while he was there same true of carson and trump and fiorina and even politicians like huckabee and jindal none of them will be president but their overthetop activism will ensure them a profitable career after politics and that assume whole point democrats have their share of bad politicians but don t see this kind of faux activism and exploitation in their party

president obamas supporters sometimes wonder where inspirational candidate of has gone answer to white house obamas presidency about smaller less inspiring questions than his campaign obamas presidency bounded by limits of office and demands of moment it about what america needs to do right now next budget next bill next years taxes last war candidates can muse presidents must govern obamas campaign was about what kind of country america how to read its past to best guide its future his speech in selma really worth reading in its entirety was among best of his presidency precisely because it had almost nothing to do with his presidency it was a return to central topic of his campaign historians who want to understand obama will find few better summations than two paragraphs core of this speech do a disservice to cause of justice by intimating that bias and discrimination are immutable or that racial division inherent to america if think nothings changed in past fifty years ask somebody who lived through selma or chicago or la of fifties ask female ceo who once might have been assigned to secretarial pool if nothings changed ask gay friend if its easier to be out and proud in america now than it was thirty years ago to deny this progress progress would be to rob us of own agency responsibility to do what can to make america better of course a more common mistake to suggest that racism banished that work that drew men and women to selma complete and that whatever racial tensions remain are a consequence of those seeking to play race card for their own purposes dont need ferguson report to know thats not true just need to open eyes and ears and hearts to know that this nations racial history still casts its long shadow upon us know march not yet over race not yet won and that reaching that blessed destination where are judged by content of character requires admitting as much those words are a precise distillation of obamas view of america and role politics must play in it first paragraph obamas case for hope america improving it has always been improving and to deny that improvement to steal from americans a belief in their country that they have more than earned to deny this progress progress would be to rob us of own agency he said second paragraph obamas case for change americas sins are not vanquished its hatreds remain real its racism still breathes know march not yet over obama said race not yet won and that reaching that blessed destination where are judged by content of character requires admitting as much hope and change these are two ideas that form steady core of obamas politics but more than that they are two ideas that define for obama what kind of country america and what it means to serve it obamas critics question his love for country he governs do not believe and know this a horrible thing to say but do not believe that president loves america former new york mayor rudy giuliani said in february they look obamas steady belief that america not yet good enough not yet pure enough not yet perfect enough and they see a skeptic not a patriot in this speech obamas answer to this criticism was direct fellow marchers so much has changed in fifty years weve endured war and fashioned peace weve seen technological wonders that touch every aspect of lives and take for granted convenience parents might scarcely imagine but what has not changed imperative of citizenship that willingness of a yearold deacon or a unitarian minister or a young mother of five to decide they loved this country so much that theyd risk everything to realize its promise thats what it means to love america thats what it means to believe in america thats what it means when say america exceptional there an implicit radicalism in what obama saying here to believe america good enough to abandon tradition of criticism and activism that has made america great obamas answer to giuliani that giuliani has mistaken uncritical adoration for hard work required of true love patriotism active not passive those who love america prove it by working to perfect america they continue marching

new york bernie sanders a crossroads vermont senator took wednesday off campaign trail home alone with his wife leaving his top aides behind in washington to cool their heels he wanted an opportunity to think said sanders senior strategist tad devine its affording him an opportunity to think about where are in campaign what he wants to say in weeks ahead he hasnt had a real chance to do that in weeks sanders campaign poured itself into new york throwing a hail mary pass to try to change delegate math while they could they spent million twice what hillary clinton did made million phone calls in final weekend alone and organized biggest rallies of a campaign defined by big rallies but in end sanders came up short not just of winning but of delegate target allies had aimed to hit might set them up for a path through california campaigns final hope now with nomination even further out of reach sanders faces difficult question about what comes next does he set a dowhateverittakes course to actually win democratic nomination or does he return to message campaign his longshot white house bid was originally seen as theres no question that sanders will stay in race either way bernie made a decision after nevada that he was going to go through this process and finish it up by letting everybody who wanted to vote vote devine said democratic primary voters have shown no sign theyre in a rush for race to settle down and seem hungry for sanders message if not his presidency hell continue to draw massive crowds from zealous fans who have almost literally given his campaign a blank check to do as they wish wednesday night campaign announced raising million more than clinton in march though they also spent much more but even some sanders allies cringed parts of candidates message in new york where his agenda was sometimes obscured by a focus clinton and issues with election process going forward what were encouraging senator sanders to do to continue to keep campaign focused issues said neil sroka communications director of democracy for america backs sanders campaign says they want to return to more substantive issues but only as long as clinton campaign joins them so far least they believe clinton forces are keeping up heat and see it as sign clinton still views sanders as a threat privately some sanders allies say its time for candidate to start to thinking more about how he maximizes his leverage democratic national convention and afterwards and less about beating clinton all costs one indicator of kind of campaign sanders wants to run how much he and his aides continue to talk about relying super delegates to hand them nomination strategy calls for wooing unelected delegates even if sanders loses more primaries and caucuses was floated by campaign manager jeff weaver with msnbcs steve kornacki late tuesday night moveonorg and democracy for america both of have endorsed sanders have since been pressuring super delegates to support whichever candidate gets most votes more than people signed petitions from group agreeing that race for democratic party nomination should be decided by who gets most votes and not who has most support from party insiders both groups confirmed to msnbc wednesday that they still hold that position moveon members overwhelmingly endorsed sanders for president and want him to win most pledged delegates become nominee and become president but superdelegates shouldnt overrule will of democratic grassroots said moveon washington director ben wikler if primary and caucus winner hillary clinton then clinton should be nominee devine said campaigns main focus still to win pledged delegates but if sanders falls just short of a majority its negligible de minims devine said and campaign will pitch super delegates that sanders stronger general election candidate another indication of sanders intentions will be where he devotes his precious time in days leading up to tuesdays contests five states are voting and maryland expected to be sanders worst showing if he invests heavily there its a sign his campaign still focused winning and scraping together delegates if he spends time in states like connecticut and delaware to try to eek out wins despite limited delegate opportunities its a sign hes more focused moral victories than ones that lead to nomination what sanders decides to do and how aggressively he decides to continuing pursuing clinton will help determine shape of rest of primary and how quickly clinton oddson nominee can unite party clintons favorability rating has tumbled during primary especially among sanders voters presumably least in part due to his attacks but as democratic leaders worry about damage party seem to be enjoying contest national polling between clinton and sanders has tightened to a virtual dead heat suggesting voters are not ready to settle clinton exit polls showed twothirds of new york primary voters found heated contest in state to be energizing not divisive mr sanderss presence has made this an immeasurably more substantive race new york times wrote in an editorial wednesday calling sanders to ignore calls to leave race was shared by sanders campaign enthusiasm of his supporters should be a wakeup call to leaders of both parties they are missing something big about their own members priorities and their mood still sanders entire campaign has been about bringing new people into politics and democratic party some point he might risk pushing them back out again

cnn veterans day recognize and honor sacrifices service members and their families make for country owe service members very best but unless congress acts january more than million veterans who rely social security benefits or pension and compensation benefits will not get their annual cost of living increase this freeze in funds has happened only two other times since will be really tough veterans among million americans who depend social security and other benefits to help make ends meet twothirds of seniors depend social security for majority of their income and for million americans social security all that stands between them and poverty while vets and seniors get no raise ceos top american companies received average a pay increase last year thats a lot of money average ceo one of top american companies made million and got more than half a million in pay raises so ceos get huge raises while seniors veterans and others whove worked hard dont get an extra dime why its not an accident its not inevitable its result of deliberate policies set by congress social security supposed to be indexed to inflation so that when prices go up benefits go up too but congress formula samples spending patterns of about a quarter of country and formula isnt geared to what older americans actually spend projections for costs of core goods and services show inflation up about but seniors wont get a cost of living increase mostly because of falling gasoline prices dont mean as much to millions of seniors who dont commute to work so seniors who are already struggling to scrape by to cover rent and exploding prescription drug prices will be left scrambling sure companies should make their own decisions about how much to compensate executives but because of laws congress has passed american taxpayers are forced to subsidize these multimilliondollar pay packages its time for congress to make different choices thats why last week introduced seniors and veterans emergency save benefits act save benefits act will give seniors social security veterans those with disabilities and others a onetime payment in equivalent to an average increase of same as taxpayersubsidized raise that ceos received last year can afford to give seniors and vets a raise in fact can increase pay for seniors and vets without adding a single penny to deficit simply by closing bonus loophole for corporate executives according to chief actuary of social security administration closing this loophole will create enough revenue to help millions of americans and still have enough left over to help extend life of social security trust fund seniors and vets would get an increase of about next year a little less than a month that increase would cover almost three months of groceries for seniors or a years worth of outofpocket costs critical prescription drugs for average medicare beneficiary that a month worth a lot to those million americans according to an analysis from economic policy institute that little boost could lift more than million americans out of poverty giving vets and seniors a little help and stitching up these corporate tax writeoffs isnt just about economics its about values for too long weve listened to a handful of people with money and power who say cut taxes for those top cut rules and regulations that keep everyone honest and let everyone else fight over scraps tried trickledown economics and it failed but can make different choices choices that reflect values dont have to ignore this problem can give a small boost to million americans who have earned it and who need it can lift over million people out of poverty can extend life of social security and can do it by shutting down taxpayer giveaways to a handful of wealthy corporations that will do fine without them for this simple spending should reflect values and that means passing save benefits act

in in highprofile supreme court gunrights case called district of columbia v heller a brief was filed from eighth floor of price daniel sr state office building in austin texas specifically from corner office of man who was then state s solicitor general ted cruz brief took a strong stance divisive question of whether second amendment establishes an individual right to own guns or just protects state and local militias brief argued forcefully for first view writing that individual right to keep and bear arms a fundamental right and that an individual right that can be altogether abrogated no right all thirty attorneys general from other states signed today with white house pushing new gun restrictions and cruzs candidacy riding next months iowa caucuses its no surprise that texas republican would embrace gun rights as a defining issue holding an event a firing range and even raffling off an engraved gauge shotgun as a campaign promotion in situation was very different he was wading into a case that had no immediate connection to texas all but to see signature of r ted cruz brief would not have surprised nine supreme court justices in least by that time cruz had been solicitor general for five years and inserting himself into a case of wide prominence and importance regardless of any direct tie to texas had become part of his playbook he clearly saw heller case as a watershed in gun rights writing that it would determine whether second amendment has any modern relevance and he was right supreme court in a decision shot down washington dc handgun ban and ruled for first time in history of this country that amendment ensures an individual person s right to have a gun for selfdefense national rifle association recognized cruz s role with a resolution as cruz climbs to top of republican presidential field fiveplus years he served as solicitor general of texas remain most important period in his public résumé they re record he ran when he was elected to us senate in and they represent significantly more of his working life than three years he has served so far in senate theyre also a prime source of fodder for liberal and moderate critics should be become republican presidential nominee a politico review of cruz s record as solicitor general shows he used role in a new and far more ideological way than his predecessors taking a relatively lowprofile job that had traditionally been used mostly to defend state government and turning it into a stage for pushing national conservative causes cruz argued eight cases in front of us supreme court far more than his predecessors and successors using each of them to advance a position endorsed by conservative thinkers he also was counsel of record some friendofthecourt briefs or amicus briefs weighing in cases across country like heller in texas had no direct stake but similarly offered a chance to argue ideological points he really turned office into a platform david bernstein a george mason law professor who recently wrote a book about obama administration for cruz wrote foreword said in an interview he built up national aura of office said tom phillips who was chief justice of supreme court of texas start of cruz s run as solicitor general cruz s zealous work served ambitions of conservative movement but he also personally used it to build a bridge between what he was an elite constitutional law nerd to what he today an ascendant rightwing politician yet while pleasing to conservatives other aspects of his record are sure to attract critics he argued against leniency for an unjustly sentenced man whose lawyer had made a technical mistake he invoked thcentury saxon law and practice of cutting off testicles to justify harsher punishments in a rape case and he referred to a lateterm abortion technique as infanticide although little examined in race so far cruz s time as solicitor general built him a powerful allegiance among conservative donors necessary to launch a national campaign heading into iowa new hampshire south carolina and beyond he has more money his disposal than any candidate besides jeb bush and way he used that texas office part of reason toby neugebauer who wrote a million check to cruz super pac called keep promise ii told reason he did that single reason what cruz did as solicitor general he said other major donors feel same way re backing someone neugebauer told that had a worldview and went out and executed it cruz took solicitor job as a reboot of sorts after his initial foray into politics had fizzled as a debater princeton as a law school student harvard and as a clerk to thensupreme court chief justice william rehnquist cruz was widely considered brilliant but he left his first real job as a promising appellate attorney in private practice in washington to instead toil in as a domestic policy analyst in george w bush s presidential campaign then as a legal hand for bush s team in frantic recount in florida that ultimately won bush election in return cruz desperately wanted a senior post in white house he wrote in his autobiography campaign though he had earned a reputation for his outsized ego as much as for his obvious intelligence and postelection jobs he got were outoftheway roles department of justice and federal trade commission just don t like guy bush has said since solicitor general role in his home state of texas offered to him by greg abbott then attorney general of texas now governor was a political lifeline from start it offered a chance to turn his legal acumen into political potential because it came with a green light to advance conservative aims in courts abbott s directive for solicitor general of texas cruz said in his book was to look across country and identify chances to defend conservative principles this not traditional definition of a solicitor general s job comes with two main duties first states get sued attorneys general and their staffs respond and some of cases get appealed and that s where solicitor general comes in representing state in higher courts where cases are most legalistic and complex second writing of amicus briefs in cases elsewhere in a state believes it has an interest even though it s not a party first part can be seen as defense second part more like offense selectively advancing interests of state way cruz did job even defense was offense and interests being asserted were less those of state than its conservative leaders texas a huge state and there s plenty of work for attorney general s office to do said jim ho an attorney in dallas who succeeded cruz as solicitor general and a friend and supporter cruz though was not content to simply do what was asked and go home when it came to cases that allowed him to argue for things like forceful application of death penalty and expressions of religion in public arena and against things like abortion and gun control ho told cruz was constant watch for opportunities to press a conservative vision of constitution

presidential campaign of sen bernie sanders of vermont filed a lawsuit against democratic national committee friday arguing that party had unfairly suspended campaign s access to key voter information after several tense hours both sides announced a deal had been reached suit came shortly after campaign manager jeff weaver acknowledged a washington news conference that sanders staffers had improperly reviewed information gathered by rival hillary clinton earlier in week but he accused dnc of over reacting to breach by suspending sanders campaign s ability to access computer system containing information about democraticleaning voters including data campaign has gathered about its own supporters after midnight sanders and dnc put out statements that both indicated impasse had been resolved but that put remarkably different spins outcome sanders s campaign said dnc had capitulated and that sanders would soon regain access to data dnc said what happened was completely unacceptable and that it would continue to investigate circumstances even as sanders regained access to valuable information without a quick resolution messy public brawl threatened to overshadow saturday s third democratic presidential debate and cast doubt dnc s ability to manage sophisticated data tools necessary for party to win white house next year and it sparked significant suspicions among sanders supporters that party was conspiring to give a boost to clinton dnc chairwoman debbie wasserman schultz rejected that allegation friday alleging that sanders staffers had exploited a software error to essentially steal data from clinton s campaign wasserman schultz said party would not allow sanders access to critical database again until his campaign agreed to an independent audit of what happened suit filed in federal court in washington argued that under a contract between dnc and campaign governing data system s use formal notice in writing required if either side believes other has violated deal in addition each side supposed to be allowed days to address any concerns suit said dnc may not suspend campaign s access to critical voter data out of haste or desperation to clean up after dnc s own mistakes suit says voter data heavily used to raise money and sanders campaign estimated that it losing a day in critical fundraising and publicity opportunities without access to files incident strained relationship between campaign of an upstart vermont senator who until this year has run as an independent and a national party his supporters have long accused of favoring clinton weaver accused party of purposely sabotaging sanders by refusing to restore access to voter information by their action leadership of democratic national recent debates over us government spying have focused one specific program national security agencys bulk collection of americans telephone records and patriot act provision that supplied programs legal justification from that perspective partial expiration of patriot act a week ago and subsequent passage of surveillance reform legislation might seem like a decisive victory against mass surveillance but surveillance debate actually a lot bigger than phone records program and patriot act nsa has other spying programs with other legal foundations and usa freedom act president obama signed tuesday doesnt do anything to rein them in friday talked to john napier tye a former state department official who resigned last year over what he regards as unconstitutional surveillance by us federal government he sees usa freedom act as a small step in right direction but hes hoping next step will be a broader debate about other ways us government spies innocent americans most people have heard about patriot act was signed just weeks after attacks and was reformed this week by usa freedom act but tye argues real action elsewhere have to keep in mind that most nsa collection americans was not under patriot act it was under other legal authorities and none of that surveillance affected by this new law he says when think about all collection that nsa has been doing americans a small percentage or percent maybe less was under patriot act tye points to two other sources of authority for government spying one fisa amendments act legal basis for a controversial nsa program called prism that collects private data from major internet companies like google and facebook other executive order a reaganera directive that has become basis for a lot of nsa spying tye says that eo an executive order its not a statute it was never passed by congress it was originally issued by president reagan in and has been amended several times since then including by george w bush a huge amount of data collected gmails and yahoo messages facebook messages apple imessages eo gives a very broad grant of authority to intelligence agencies to do all kinds of things tye says theres one section in there that allows nsa to collect data us persons as part of a lawful foreign intelligence investigation that provision being used to collect a huge amount of americans communications and data when government collects information overseas it isnt bound by most us surveillance laws nsa has reserved right to incidentally collect americans private communications overseas so long as americans are not target of a particular surveillance effort but tye argues that this a huge loophole in practice dont think most americans understand that almost all of their internet and phone data either stored backup servers overseas or transits outside of borders he says a huge amount of data collected under this authority outside borders of us gmails and yahoo messages facebook messages apple imessages twitter every service can think of tye says he has firsthand knowledge of extent of nsa spying programs thats because he was an official in state departments bureau of democracy human rights and labor in when edward snowdens revelations became public jobs was internet freedom promoting free and open internet around world tye says when snowden leaks happened in june of was not surprised knew that a lot of stuff had been happening knew that a lot of surveillance was going that no one knew about and it didnt surprise that this was finally frontpage news congress should take a more active role in overseeing executive branch activity tye says that after snowden leaks part of job was to help develop responses to initiatives within un human rights council and un general assembly by germany brazil and other countries that were very concerned with online privacy to be able to properly negotiate those efforts was briefed scope of us intelligence practices in two different classified briefings one in fall of and one in first part of learned about activities under tye believed these programs were unconstitutional and began filing complaints inside government about them he met with intelligence committees in both house and senate and filed complaints with inspectors general of both state department and national security agency he says none of them were very interested in issue so last summer tye quit his job and went public with his concerns in an oped for washington post he says he ran oped through government censors to make sure it didnt disclose any classified information tye has spent last year public interest group avaaz and now starting his own law firm that will focus civil rights and human rights issues tye would like president obama to unilaterally revise executive order to better protect privacy of americans if obama doesnt tye would like congress to hold hearings issue think that congress should take a more active role in overseeing executive branch activity under tye says congress should hold hearings and find out what protections really exist for americans and how much data being collected he also favors reforms to fisa amendments act was passed by congress in whereas some provisions of patriot act were set to expire this year and have now been renewed until fisa amendments act permanent most controversial part of fisa amendments act section allows warrantless surveillance to collect americans communications so long as target a foreigner located overseas under section nsa collecting a lot of stuff inside united states thats nominally targeting foreigners but in fact scooping up innocent communications from hundreds of millions of americans tye says even worse fbi has access to that database and can search it for domestic criminal reasons so purpose of law nominally was to allow nsa to conduct foreign intelligence investigations but as its currently being used fbi using this data for domestic criminal investigations protections that normally want in place for domestic criminal investigations are not necessarily there

cnn conservatives dissatisfied with donald trump have given libertarian presidential nominee a chance to take his third party to center stage but gary johnson has not exactly been a fixture campaign trail largely eschewing traditional campaign events former new mexico governor has instead opted to appear almost daily multiple news programs and appeal to his solid core of online support that could soon change as campaign considers more regular appearances in real world johnson campaign spokesperson joe hunter told cnn monday campaign now undergoing a natural evolution having recently ramped up its fundraising efforts with stated intention of buying advertisements in key states and beginning to hold rallies across country despite enjoying a brief surge in leadup to major party conventions johnson has generally hovered in high single digits in national polls latest cnn poll of polls showed johnson support nationwide in a race where majority of voters say they are dissatisfied with their mainstream offerings for president where republican nominee has secured little support from nations largest generation millennials and where digital media has taken an unprecedented place in political landscape heres what johnson and isnt doing to make his long shot candidacy a viable bet hunter called constant interviews a critical component of campaign and after taking scheduling into account take every opportunity get similar to trump johnson and his running mate bill weld have relied earned media in form of appearances in press unlike trump two almost always stay message since winning nomination libertarian partys convention in may johnson and weld a former massachusetts governor have used their appearances to cast themselves as centrist candidates between trump and hillary clinton they point out their experience as republican governors in traditionally democraticleaning states johnson often touts his laissezfaire approach to drugs and immigration while weld has pointed to his record lgbt rights and his cordial relationship with both sides of aisle including clintons both champion their conservative credos matters of spending but live television appearances including two primetime town hall events cnn have not yet afforded libertarian ticket national recognition or a strong showing in polls digital side johnson campaign has largely stuck to its roots by embracing libertarian community online reaching supporters through social media and email campaign has kept its base abreast of its progress and appealed for funds johnson campaign said tuesday its august money bomb fundraising campaign surpassed its goal of million based off entirely digital appeals for money campaign said it raised monday alone totaling million in donations in first two weeks of month a number of groups have fallen into place doing some of heavy lifting that long shot bid needs doing among most standout recent entries into johnson orbit a newly minted group called republicans for johnsonweld group formed this month to bring gop leaders and voters trump had alienated into libertarian fold republican strategist and group spokesperson liz mair told cnn monday projohnson forces seeking to convert republicans from a divisive nominee were in uncharted waters dont think anybodys tried to launch an effort like this mair said despite lack of an established path johnson has notched a few victories rep scott rigell said he will support johnson in november and reps reid ribble and mike coffman have said they are considering it in separate interviews hunter and mair both said they had heard supportive words from republican politicians but that many were not ready to come out into open against their party outside group could be a key factor in mainstreaming of johnson it already holds several wellknown republican staffers monday it added joseph russo iii a former sarah palin aide mair said given nature of group mair said a number of us have better connections to gop capitol hill figures than libertarian campaign what will mitt romney do within johnson campaign weld has taken lead outreach to republicans however his biggest prize still eludes him republican nominee mitt romney romney served as governor of massachusetts after weld and has said if weld were topping ticket he would be supporting them but he still needed to hear what johnson had to say months have passed since then without a romney endorsement libertarian efforts to sway republicans have also had to push back against notion they are helping clinton johnson has said he pulling about evenly from both sides and been forceful in his condemnation of trump mair said trump was doing work of helping clinton his own and that clintons win was this point overwhelming likely although johnson still had a chance in an unpredictable cycle republicans screwed up thats not gary johnsons fault she said up in air hunter said many people would not come to their side until johnson candidacy had reached a critical mass they hope to achieve by ramping up their campaign efforts and their ad spending in addition to television ads campaign said it was looking radio billboards and continuing its online efforts as with major party candidates outside spending groups have also taken it upon themselves to launch ads in support of johnson a libertarian spending group called americans deserve better pac has put together a few spots hailing former governors as adults in race republicans for johnsonweld meanwhile said its focus less traditional advertising than generating viral content so far however one of groups most viral moments came not from distributed content but from an appearance cnn ahead of second libertarian town hall mair told cnns anderson cooper that trumps message being a loudmouthed d basically all told this third party effort has so far expounded more resources general election advertisements than trumps campaign has yet to join in air wars all of these efforts have so far been dwarfed by clinton and her allied super pacs have spent exponentially more than three other presidential campaigns johnson has not been able to draw kind of rock star crowd sizes that trump or vermont sen bernie sanders attracted earlier this year former new mexico governor held one rally in utah a saturday earlier this month and another in reno nevada after rescheduling he set to hold another in albuquerque new mexico this saturday his campaign said it was still working out specifics other rallies in near future but that it planned to host public events soon in florida and new england meanwhile fundraising efforts have come largely from digital side although campaign said governors have appealed directly for funds with a planned event in miami wednesday their most prominent fundraising event so far came in late july a hollywood event hosted by drew carey libertarian host of price right

whether an october surprise may have come a month early it s too soon to say but hillary clinton s weekend health scare already has started to stir speculation about whether democratic officials should be discussing possibility of a plan b in case they need to hastily arrange for a replacement nominee raises a basic question how does that work democratic party bylaws say dnc has power to fill vacancies in nominations for office of president and vice president when national convention not in session under party rules dnc chair currently donna brazile could call a special meeting and fill vacancy by a majority vote of those present analysts still see this scenario as exceedingly unlikely it appears party leaders have no authority to sideline clinton meaning special meeting would kick in only if she were to step aside voluntarily nominee and her aides say shes recovering and feeling much better now but after clinton left a memorial sunday stumbling and being helped into an suv by multiple aides an incident campaign tied to a recent pneumonia diagnosis and other factors some democrats are least thinking about that process former dnc chairman don fowler told politico that party needs to develop a plan immediately for finding a potential successor candidate former al jazeera and msnbc anchor david shuster tweeted sunday that dnc was considering an emergency meeting to talk about a clinton replacement although his source emphasized her status as nominee was up to her not party npr s cokie roberts said monday that democrats already are considering another candidate it has them very nervously beginning to whisper about having her step aside and finding another candidate roberts said adding that it s unlikely to be a real thing and describing talk as likely an overreaction perhaps inadvertently feeding that frenzy former ohio democratic gov ted strickland introduced clinton running mate tim kaine an event monday by describing him as ready to become president if necessary columbus dispatch reported but in albeit unlikely scenario of a replacement scramble kaine isn t necessarily a shooin vermont sen bernie sanders vice president joe biden massachusetts sen elizabeth warren and others could all be possible candidates historian doug wead told fox news neil cavuto that votes would tend to go toward whomever top of ticket endorses though it s not guaranteed whatever clinton says will likely hold sway but legally technically they have their own vote he said replacing a member of a majorparty ticket would be exceedingly rare though not unprecedented this happened in when democratic nominee george mcgovern s running mate sen thomas eagleton withdrew from race in july after it was revealed he had undergone electric shock treatment for mental health issues eagleton was replaced in august by sargent shriver as new vice presidential nominee mcgovernshriver ticket went to lose race in a landslide to incumbent president richard nixon rick hasen who runs election law blog told foxnewscom that situation could become significantly more complicated if it were to play out later in calendar more complicated problem would arise if ballots have already been printed and people would have started voting there electoral college would come into play and electors from party would be expected to vote for replacement nominee hasen said first absentee ballots were mailed out in sept in north carolina and many other states will soon follow by midseptember deadlines for both parties to certify candidates for general election will have passed in every state meaning lawsuits might have to be filed to try and get a replacement ballot dnc did not respond to a request for comment from foxnewscom this isn t first time this election cycle there has been talk of a candidate dropping out in august during a particularly rough patch of poll numbers for trump some gop officials were reportedly considering contingency plans in case republican nominee dropped out adam shaw a politics reporter and occasional opinion writer for foxnewscom he can be reached here or twitter adamshawny

raul a reyes an attorney and member of usa today board of contributors follow him twitter raulareyes opinions expressed in this commentary are solely those of author cnn as nation was preparing for july fourth weekend there was grim news from san francisco wednesday night kate steinle was fatally shot apparently randomly while walking with her father a busy pier a mexican immigrant who cnn reported was in country without documentation was arrested in her death illegally reentering country after being deported as juan francisco lopezsanchez said to have done a federal felony he has also been accused of a horrific violent crime and according to immigration authorities he has seven other felony convictions including four for drug offenses but that doesnt make him a symbol of estimated million undocumented immigrants in united states nor he poster boy for outofcontrol illegal immigration across southern border illegal immigration from mexico a year low he does not represent overwhelming majority of immigrants in this country legal or otherwise who are productive members of society lopezsanchez simply a dangerous individual who should not have been free and among us it a myth that increased illegal immigration leads to more crime research from immigration policy center shows that crime rates fell in united states as size of immigrant population including undocumented immigrants grew from to most undocumented immigrants come to united states to work and provide a better life for themselves and their families consider that several mass shootings from aurora to newtown to charleston were committed by young white men does that mean that all young white men are potential mass murderers of course not same news outlets that are now trumpeting wednesdays murder as proof that undocumented immigrants are criminals often overlook or ignore other stories of undocumented immigrants who are genuine heroes in an undocumented immigrant rescued a mother and her child staten island new york amidst storm surge of superstorm sandy one takeaway from this episode that deporting as many undocumented immigrants as possible not answer to immigration problems lopezsanchez had been deported five times and yet he was still here in country without authorization back in deputy director of immigration and customs enforcement told an immigration subcommittee of congress that it costs to deport a person multiply this by five and that how much taxpayer money was wasted a criminal who remained large to randomly take life of an innocent young woman another lesson here country does not need more immigration enforcement country needs smarter and better immigration enforcement up to now immigration authorities have wasted time manpower and money chasing after people working productively in their communities as say gardeners and maids while felons like lopezsanchez slipped through cracks thats why it was good news last week that department of homeland security announced it rethinking its deportation priorities to focus recent arrivals and serious criminals this move a step in right direction because it time to start seriously targeting those immigrants who are a real threat to public safety government will be focusing its enforcement efforts three categories of people convicted criminals recent border crossers and terrorism threats true lopezsanchez should not have been in country or he should have least been behind bars but immigration and customs enforcement erred in not seeking a warrant or court order for his arrest and he was released in accordance with city law in march whats more president barack obamas proposed executive action immigration currently tied up in a legal battle might also have made a difference because it would have freed up resources to go after people like lopezsanchez although steinles death was a tragedy it was alleged action of one man all undocumented immigrants do not deserve to be vilified by false association

prime minister benjamin netanyahu sunday said he would not allow israel to be submerged by refugees after calls for jewish state to take in those fleeing syrias war speaking weekly cabinet meeting netanyahu also announced start of construction of a fence along israels border with jordan according to his office will not allow israel to be submerged by a wave of illegal migrants and terrorist activists netanyahu said israel not indifferent to human tragedy of syrian and african refugees but israel a small country very small without demographic or geographic depth that why must control borders opposition leader isaac herzog saturday said israel should take in syrian refugees recalling plight of jews who sought refuge from past conflicts palestinian president mahmud abbas also called for israel to allow palestinians from refugee camps in syria to travel to palestinian territories whose external borders are controlled by jewish state there already hostility in israel toward asylumseekers from africa and a concerted government effort to repatriate them rights groups say thousands of african asylum seekers have been coerced into voluntary departures official figures show illegal immigrants are in israel almost all from eritrea and sudan most of those not in detention live in poor areas of southern tel aviv where there have been several protests against them start of construction of kilometer mile fence announced by netanyahu involves extension of a security barrier to part of its eastern border with jordan in a bid to keep out militants and illegal migrants netanyahu said when it was approved in june that new fence was a continuation of a kilometer barrier built along egyptian border blocked entry of illegal migrants into israel and various terrorist movements in its first stage new fence being built along israels eastern border between eilat and where a new airport will be built in timna valley will continue fence up to golan heights netanyahu said that would take it into israelioccupied west bank along jordan valley an area already under israeli military control but claimed by palestinians as part of their state israel has insisted maintaining troops in area in any final peace agreement a stance completely rejected by palestinians who say it would be a violation of their sovereignty and merely perpetuate occupation israel also has a fence that runs along syrian frontier through israelioccupied golan heights those fences are in addition to a barrier that runs through west bank israel began building during second palestinian intifada or uprising lasted from israel seized square kilometers square miles of golan from syria in six day war and annexed it years later in a move never recognized by international community

geoengineering unlikely to work conservation group says posted nov by alex kirby climate news network biofilm used in research into carbon capture doubts persist about geoengineering energygov via wikimedia commons london global watchdog responsible for protecting world s wealth of species un s convention biological diversity cbd has looked hopes for reining in climate change through geoengineering its bleak conclusion echoing that reached by many independent scientists that chances are highly uncertain novel means in this context describes trying to reduce greenhouse gas ghg emissions by removing them from atmosphere and altering amount of heat from sun that reaches earth some scientists and policymakers say geoengineering as these strategies are collectively known essential if world to meet goals of paris agreement this because current attempts to reduce emissions cannot make big enough cuts fast enough to keep global average temperatures from rising more than c above their preindustrial levels agreement s basic goal but cbd says in a report that geoengineering while it could possibly help to prevent world overheating might endanger global biodiversity and have other unpredictable effects many independent analysts have raised similar concernsattempts to increase amount of carbon in oceans in order to remove ghgs have so far shown disappointing results one report doubted that geoengineering could slow sealevel rise another said it could not arrest melting of arctic ice a third study found that geoengineering would make things little better and might even make global warming worse transboundary impacts lead author of cbd geoengineering report a british scientist dr phillip williamson of uk s natural environment research council he an associate fellow in school of environmental sciences university of east anglia uk cbd originally became involved in climate geoengineering in because member governments were concerned that experiments to fertilise oceans could pose unknown risks to environment they were then unregulated when carried out in international waters cbd s concern expanded to include other geoengineering techniques especially atmospheric methods could have uncertain transboundary impacts some scientists argue that geoengineering a hazilydefined term and prefer to speak instead simply of greenhouse gas removal dr williamson and his colleagues say assessment of impacts of geoengineering biodiversity not straightforward and subject to many uncertainties greenhouse gas removal they warn that removing a given quantity of a greenhouse gas would not fully compensate for an earlier overshoot of emissions new risks in some cases they say cure may be worse than disease largescale deployment of bioenergy with carbon capture and storage beccs seems likely to have significant negative impacts biodiversity through land use change when it comes to attempts to reflect sunlight back out into space or to manage solar radiation a familiar theme recurs there are high levels of uncertainty about impacts of srm solar radiation management techniques could present significant new risks to biodiversity time and again it seems a potential advance liable to be cancelled by an equally likely reverse if srm benefits coral reefs by decreasing temperatureinduced bleaching as it may in certain conditions it may also increase indirectly impacts of ocean acidification there could even be a risk in some circumstances of loss to earth s protective ozone layer dr williamson and his colleagues believe that geoengineering essential if it can be made to work because of diminishing chances that anything else will m sceptical that s not to say bioenergy with carbon capture and storage impossible but it seems extremely unlikely to be feasible they write it may still be possible that deep and very rapid decarbonisation by all countries might allow climate change to be kept within a c limit by emission reduction alone however any such window of opportunity rapidly closing repeatedly those two words recur a suggested technique or development will be highly uncertain most of report amounts to a very cautious call for more research coupled with an implicit acceptance that in end geoengineering unlikely to prove capable of contributing much to climate mitigation dr williamson told climate news network m sceptical that s not to say bioenergy with carbon capture and storage impossible but it seems extremely unlikely to be feasible for all sorts of reasons scale needed when cbd member governments meet in december they are expected to call for more research a safe option in most circumstances but far from a ringing endorsement of a technology once seen as very promising alex kirby a former bbc journalist and environment correspondent he now works with universities charities and international agencies to improve their media skills and with journalists in developing world keen to specialise in environmental reporting advertisement

sunday nights democratic debate comes a crucial moment two frontrunners hillary clinton and bernie sanders are closer in polls than ever and iowa caucus only two weeks away up until now clinton has done well in debates its a format that suits her but sanders message breaking through and hes gaining in polls nationally leads in new hampshire and neck and neck with clinton in iowa that means pressure for both candidates sunday night debate taking place in charleston sc begins pm est nbcyoutubecongressional black caucus institute debate will air nbc broadcast stations and livestreamed nbc digital platforms including its youtube channel heres what candidates need to do and avoid needs show why her plans are pragmatic solutions that work for progressive base while still respecting sanders supporters shes gotten away from her answer in first debate that shes a progressive who likes to get things done then again its tough to make that argument to primary voters avoid lawyerly unclear answers that play into an inauthentic narrative especially paired against sanders whose authenticity has been key attacks that could be seen as cheap shots like health care needs convince democratic voters who are skeptical hes presidential material that he can win show a facility with issues beyond his core message avoid pulling punches like hes done in past debates needs somehow appear relevant as it he barely made it onto debate stage his poll numbers had to be rounded up to qualify avoid being dismissed especially after question investigation into his discounted purchase of furniture from governors office when he left where does race stand hillary clinton seemed to have a comfortable lead vermont independent bernie sanders last year but that has slipped considerably and they are in a neckandneck race sanders has picked up momentum with his message and his passion and democratic voters like him he continues to lead in new hampshire where hes been in top spot for better part of last five months hes also closing in iowa perhaps more surprising but thats a place where passion counts because of how caucuses are conducted beyond first two states clinton still has leads with nonwhite voters who are critical to clintons firewall in south but that firewall might not look as insurmountable if she loses one or both of these first crucial contests what about issues whats dividing these two sanders has gained steam because of his clear message wall street and income inequality hes tried to tie clinton to big financial institutions and clinton has struggled in defending those ties as sanders has labeled her status quo lately its been health care and guns sanders voted for obamacare but he prefers a singlepayer system clinton wants more modest tweaks it may have been a bad tactic for clintons daughter chelsea to try and go after sanders from left and accuse him of wanting to dismantle affordable care act clinton stood by her daughter bigger picture clinton has criticized sanders for how to pay all of reforms he wants sanders has not been completely forthcoming a financial and tax plan though he promises to explain later clinton has harped sanders past support for more moderate gun measures including his support for a law that protects gun manufacturers from liability if a gun made by one of them used in a killing saturday day before debate sanders came out in favor of a measure to amend law so with these dividing lines do they go negative in debate theres a risk in going negative but more so for clinton sanders voters are very loyal clinton losing ground and cant afford to be completely positive but how to attack sanders in a way that resonates and seems authentic will be a challenge clintons making case shes more electable and certainly most democratic voters would do anything to make sure a republican isnt elected especially ted cruz or donald trump

watch live coverage of ceremonies in selma commemorating years since bloody sunday starting am et saturday cnn and cnngo cnn fifty years ago this weekend a yearold john lewis was beaten so badly by alabama state troopers that they fractured his skull lewis calls edmund pettus bridge where troopers and and a group of white men deputized into a posse by sheriff attacked hundreds of peaceful protesters bloody sunday march an almost holy place now a democratic us congressman lewis returning to selma as he has nearly every year since that historic march to remember fight for voting rights and to push voters across country to participate in political process he also wants people to continue to speak up about problems of racial injustice and poverty that persist in american society it important to come back to remember selma lewis told cnn in an interview first baptist church vote powerful it most powerful nonviolent tool have in a democratic society and dont want people to forget that people paid a price selma these churches and these people gave it everything they had wouldnt be where are today as a nation and as a people if it hadnt been for this community churches like selmas first baptist and brown chapel served as important meeting places during movement in part because they were run by black community and music was vital as a unifying force without music movement would have been like a bird without wings lewis said he recalled how people would often sing during marches sometimes making up songs along way music helped create a sense of solidarity and there were people in some communities in some towns and cities like selma saying if meetings are being held church it must be all right thats house of lord they must be doing something that right activists had been working for years in and around selma trying to help people register to vote time only of blacks in dallas county were registered lewis said when blacks attempted to register they were often given impossible tasks like counting number of jelly beans in a jar or number of bubbles a bar of soap to complete before being allowed to sign up black lawyers doctors business people public school teachers and college professors were told they could not read and write well enough to pass socalled literacy test as chairman of student nonviolent coordinating committee lewis along with many others would spill blood to change that not only as a leader of bloody sunday march but in other confrontations across south lewis was arrested some times during s as he challenged segregationist jim crow laws that kept blacks oppressed he spent days imprisoned in mississippi during freedom rides in as a student fisk university he organized sitins lunch counters in nashville tennessee even spending his birthday in jail there in so lewis was no stranger to runins with law but events of bloody sunday would prove fateful for movement television cameras and photographers captured troopers attacking marchers with night sticks and whips one man in posse even had a rubber hose wrapped with barbed wire lewis wrote in his memoir walking with wind troopers trampled crowd with their horses and released nauseating tear gas pictures horrified citizens across country eight days after march president lyndon b johnson spoke before congress about voting rights he signed voting rights act aug to ensure that all citizens would be able to vote regardless of their skin color five decades later lewis said a great deal of progress has been made in struggle for equality but he added that despite having twice elected a black man to nations highest office country still has a long way to go to become socalled beloved community dr martin luther king jr envisioned where can lay down burden of race have a black president weve made a lot of progress but were not a postracial society yet he said still have a distance to go scars and stains of racism are still deeply embedded in american society need for righteous indignation lewis who has written a trilogy of graphic novels for young adults about his life called march said it was important not to sweep problems that still plague america under rug and to instead speak up with a sense of righteous indignation cannot be quiet have to speak up have to speak out have to find a way to get in way and make some noise he said referring in particular to killings of unarmed black men by police last summer in ferguson missouri and new york city he urged those demonstrating against police brutality and racial injustice to study and model nonviolent methods of civil rights activists of s and s poverty affecting people of all races across country still gnaws lewis as does a lack of access to a good education he believes people should be outraged about hunger and violence and fired up to renew and strengthen voting rights act after a supreme court ruling gutted key provision requiring certain states with a history of racial discrimination polls to preclear any changes to voting laws with federal government before implementing them saturday lewis and nearly members of congress from both parties will join president barack obama bridge in selma a bridge that still bears name of a confederate general who was also a ku klux klan leader to mark th anniversary of bloody sunday bipartisan event comes as efforts to update to voting rights former republican marijuanasmoking everest mountaineering exgovernor of new mexico and presidential nominee of libertarian party has a problem he s barred from presidential debates to appear stage with hillary clinton and donald trump this autumn gary johnson needs to boost his national polling numbers to from around now without that national exposure and blockbuster ratings three scheduled clintontrump dustups are likely to produce it s hard for anyone to see how johnson or either of two other minorparty candidates green party s jill stein or even evan mcmullin a yearold former cia counterterrorism officer could ever become more than mere electoral curiosities but emergence of three independent candidates during a year of record dissatisfaction with major party candidates may still make for an unpredictable twist to story polling data suggests libertarians both sides of political divide are giving independent candidates a second look and in las vegas friday johnson and stein spoke before a gathering of asian american and pacific islander voters to press their cases softspoken and wearing nikes johnson presented a platform of social libertarianism fiscal conservatism and noninterventionism in foreign affairs he argued gathering that in this craziest election of all time he had a chance might be next president of united states a justreleased wsjnbc poll gave johnson in crucial swing state of colorado and stein in las vegas he was asked repeatedly if a vote for him or any thirdparty candidate was somehow wasted a wasted vote voting for somebody don t believe in that s a wasted vote johnson said vote for person believe in that s how bring about change hope after having made pitch today that ll realize if want to waste vote clinton or trump have it stein who spoke separately event similarly argued that many americans were looking for an alternative to trump and hillary clinton whom she dubbed as most disliked and untrusted majorparty nominees in us history democracy needs a moral compass she told a gathering of asian american and pacific island groups it s not just about who don t like most or who are most afraid of stein who like johnson represented her party in has outlined policy positions that include a green new deal focused renewable energy jobs and aimed making united states transition to renewable energy complete by stein has also proposed a reduction in military budget by a third and creation of a regional food systems based sustainable organic agriculture republican side evan mcmullin a beneficiary of grounded never trump movement failed to get ballot in states before he d even sent out his first campaign release mcmullin nonetheless tried to give disaffected conservatives an alternative to republican nominee who he says has tapped into people s darkest prejudices and deepest fears mcmullin s best hope and likely his only one to make an electoral dent would require trump s departure from race though nominee has made no overt sign that he wants to quit like millions of americans had hoped this year would bring us better nominees who despite party differences could offer compelling visions of a better future mcmullin said in his announcement instead have been left with two candidates who are fundamentally unfit for profound responsibilities they seek but can they begin to make a difference only once in recent election cycles have third party candidates had any significant influence when in texan businessman ross perot took of vote carving into support for incumbent republican george hw bush and helping arkansas governor bill clinton to victory some democrats blamed thirdparty candidate ralph nader for delivering george w bush election in but thirdparty candidate won a paltry of vote and his supporters were split between republicans and democrats johnson s running mate former massachusetts governor william weld said he d seen interest and fundraising pick up as trump s campaign has floundered their campaign recently reported a m oneweek fundraising haul and has reported that more than people have pledged to donate least to his campaign august but that was in a relatively tight race with clinton now leading trump by double digits in some key battleground states impact of any thirdparty candidate could be limited moment it doesn t look like they can have much impact all said hank sheinkopf a veteran democratic adviser but if race gets closer they could throw it either way sheinkopf reasoned that this election has become more about personalities and less about what party leaders want longterm impact may be a significant shift in how people view democrats and republicans he said as it now stands stein johnson and mcmullin could act as a convenient parking spot for voters disgusted with both candidates earlier this month a federal judge rejected a challenge brought by johnson and stein arguing that bar for inclusion set artificially high yet there are signs ground could be shifting mike mccurry now cochair of commission presidential debates and a former press secretary for bill clinton hinted that a third podium might be needed some of production people may have said just in case need to plan out what that might look like mccurry told politico won t know number of invitations extend until midseptember ron faucheux president of clarus research group a nonpartisan polling company said there was broad support for including even minor candidates polls show that voters think third party candidates should be included he said and in an election like this where polls show a majority of voters dislike both main party candidates there a good reason to give them opportunity to least look other options but even johnson and stein were somewhat wistful about their prospects casting themselves as agents of change who best could represent beginning of a break with current twoparty system biggest message consider us as a very very viable alternative to this twoparty system that has become so polarized that they re not able to do anything johnson has said stein advanced a similar argument re having a political reorganization in this election because republicans are kind of falling apart she said and democrats have kind of split with a lot of bernie sanders supporters just not happy with alternative she argued that it was by elections about lesser of two evils that country inherited many of its problems but first two have to get tv there s no way m going to win presidency if m not in presidential debates johnson said do believe anything possible given that right now arguably two most polarizing figures in american politics today are running for office

republican presidential contest not regardless of what it seems some days all about donald trump there s another dynamic unfolding that has almost nothing to do with businessmanpolitician currently atop polls but that will have a major influence who becomes party s nominee this other struggle involves competition among former florida governor jeb bush sen marco rubio fla and ohio gov john kasich history suggests that whoever emerges triumphant in this threeway rivalry will be in a strong position to claim nomination though admittedly past has been a poor predictor of events so far in this campaign ever since trump surged to top of polls other candidates have been trying to assess both his staying power and costbenefit analysis of engaging him trump and bush have clashed almost from start with growing intensity more recently as rubio has risen trump has taken aim him and rubio has responded in kind none of other candidates has a clear strategy for taking down trump but they all think he will look like a different candidate and in their assessments a less formidable candidate once field narrows to three or four finalists after voting begins so they are beginning to focus one another as much as they are worrying about him with first contests still months away none of three yet looks like a frontrunner in average of recent national polls rubio and bush run fourth and fifth behind trump retired neurosurgeon ben carson and former hewlettpackard chief executive carly fiorina neither bush nor rubio breaks double digits kasich doesn t even break percent national polls this stage are less meaningful than state polls in iowa where first caucus will take place in early february trump and carson lead with sen ted cruz tex currently third bush and rubio trail first three and kasich even deeper in pack in new hampshire trump also has a big lead but kasich jockeying with fiorina for second with carson and bush next and rubio farther back trump and bush take rubio in new hampshire in recent days bush rubio and kasich have shown how much they re worrying about one another they ve been sniping each other and making other moves that underscore significance of their competition rubio has long emphasized that party needs a fresh candidate not one tied to past an implicit criticism of his fellow floridian who part of an american political dynasty bush a twoterm former governor has belittled rubio s experience or lack thereof kasich a twoterm governor and longtime house member has claimed that his experience and record are unmatched by any of other candidates advisers to three anticipate more attacks ahead bush campaign feverishly doing their opposition research governor kasich and senator rubio said john weaver kasich s chief strategist an empire like that not going to go quietly into night re expecting pretty sharp elbows to be thrown re going to handle it head past republican nomination contests often have devolved into competition between a candidate from centerright or mainstream conservative wing of party and a candidate from hard right or populist conservative wing most times candidate from mainstream conservative wing becomes nominee this year race more scrambled because of added factor of apparent desire by many republicans for an outsider or nonpolitician that has elevated trump carson and fiorina and has forced others to adapt rubio has been stressing that despite being in senate he s really not of washington instead of establishment vs tea party one gop strategist describes race this time as a competition between those in anger or antiwashington lane vs those in aspirational lane bush rubio and kasich all fall more into aspirational lane what will make difference based how three candidates are running it s clear that they see path ahead in slightly different ways though each has handicaps he must overcome to win bush has repeatedly pushed back trump by arguing that anger and insults cannot win presidency he seeks to be aspirational candidate conservative enough because of his record in florida to be acceptable to a conservative party while offering a positive and inclusive message that reaches beyond gop coalition but many republicans see bush as least able to appeal across entire party not much more able to appeal to hard right than cruz would be able to attract mainstream conservatives lodged firmly in establishment wing as son and brother of former presidents he faces resistance far right and among those yearning for an outsider his hope that he can change perceptions of himself outlast his rivals with superior resources and persuade republicans that he s their best hope to win a general election sally bradshaw bush s senior adviser said key remains what it has been from start of campaign to portray bush as a conservative reformer by stressing what he did in florida people don t know that yet she said when that message burns in his numbers are going to change that s his path what pundits said then and now about donald trump kasich looking to traditional model he compassionate conservative of who hopes to strike first in new hampshire and build from there his advisers believe that eventually he can reach across divide in party to become nominee but party has not only moved right in past decade it also has developed a harder edge than when george w bush ran as a compassionate conservative in kasich s support for expanding medicaid under affordable care act just one example of a position that will not sit well with many conservatives rubio s team sees crosscutting appeal as vital a race that will favor a candidate who can best unite a fractured party senator s goal to demonstrate skills as a communicator to show depth issues to turn his personal story into a positive message for party to make as few errors as possible and over time generate enthusiasm across gop coalition rubio too has vulnerabilities his past support for a path to citizenship for undocumented immigrants from he has backed away remains an obstacle in his path so too does his personal profile that of a youthful firstterm senator with limited experience trying to become president a profile not unlike that of president obama when he first ran eight years ago david axelrod who was obama s chief strategist in both campaigns often has said that voters look for a replacement rather than a replica in picking a new president adviser to one of rubio s rivals put it this way when was last time this country elected two presidents with similar attributes rubio will be trying to dissuade his fellow republicans that he isn t another obama there are wild cards in calculations of all three camps maybe new jersey gov chris christie who occupies similar space will catch fire in new hampshire and elsewhere although resistance to him within party significant fiorina has demonstrated fearlessness that has jarred even trump and can appeal across party carson remains a candidate of unknown potential last there trump factor and what his support represents for now he remains dominant force in gop race but advisers to bush rubio and kasich see a turn in campaign heading into final months of year one that will heighten competition among them with significant consequences for their party
