friday speaker john boehner announced that he will resign from speakership and us house end of october here mischiefs of faction writers offer their quick reactions think boehners resignation more notable for when it occurred than for that it occurred he had been speaker or minority leader for almost a decade and clearly no longer relished his job think that next speaker will face same obstacles that confronted boehner particularly a no compromise faction of conservatives they cannot be satisfied with policy substance since they mostly care about tactical extremism they have also made it clear that they have little interest in sort of pork barrel politics that members traditionally have seen as a means to reelection dont think this situation will change until there a republican in white house since tea partiers object to slightest accommodation of president obama or any democratic successor theres also a whole industry now devoted to feeding conservatives belief that their leadership has sold them out this includes talk radio bloggers and groups such as heritage action perhaps a leader with a warmer relationship with movement conservatives might be a more effective speaker im skeptical that much will change soon john boehner has finally decided to take his ball and go home of course understand why he resigning house gop ungovernable right now thus while it an understandable decision it not a selfless one as several members have described it no this boehners coup de grace executed with deftness now free to rely democratic votes when necessary he will be able to keep government open in same way he might be able to pass other legislation in next month more importantly he presents his fractured caucus with a parting gift of their own making selecting their next leader beyond obvious question of who will be next speaker probably majority leader kevin mccarthy of california really interesting questions raised by boehners decision include how process will work out will other republicans step forward and challenge mccarthy for job will gop caucus ultimately come together behind a single candidate if not what will democrats do as unexpected and exciting as todays surprise announcement this probably going to get much more interesting before all said and done john boehners resignation as speaker of house a good illustration of how american political parties are strong and how they are not us political parties are not strong in topdown sense neither presidents nor congressional leaders have a lot of control over backbenchers in their parties main reason that these leaders dont control party nominations while parties have become stronger since s they have become stronger from bottom up not top down presidents dont have much better ability to force legislators to do what they want now when party line votes are norm than they did in s s and s when party line votes were rarer what changed power to shape legislative behavior comes from bottom up not just from ordinary voters although those are influential but also from organized interest groups and wealthy donors in legislators district and in their partys national constituency these factions are what really influence legislators and create party unity not their party leaders in washington so why there so much party unity now when there wasnt in s now parties are sorted ideologically all legislators who come from conservative districts and are backed by conservative interests groups are republicans while all those from liberal districts and backed by liberal interest groups are democrats so unlike s a party leader now presides over an ideologically unified caucus that usually votes together yet that doesnt mean that he or she has much more direct power over them than leaders did in decades after world war ii speakers jobs were actually more secure in post world war ii era when they led ideologically diverse parties and party control rarely changed hands last speaker of house to retire by choice because of age was tip oneill who left in all subsequent speakers have left because of internal party revolts personal scandals or electoral disappointments it a challenging job because while us political parties are strong power largely does not rest in congressional leadership politicians are loyal to their parties voters and organized factions not to their legislative leaders in dc longstanding tension within congressional parties that legislators need to cooperate with their parties to accomplish their shared goals but to do so they must overcome inherent diversity of american parties since house gop has provided a brilliant demonstration of this tension rules of house and republican conference provided boehner with a great deal of power but his majority included a sizable number of legislators who would not or could not cooperate with their party they had campaigned against party status quo they promised not to vote for compromises they promised outcomes they could not realistically achieve with a majority in one chamber eg repealing obamacare they feared a primary challenge from right more than losing to a democrat it likely that next speaker will suffer from same challenge because challenge systemic best chance to end cycle however would be for house gop to select someone who trusted by tea party faction both inside and outside of congress so that when she says thats a stupid strategy that will fail resulting in a drop in polls and a humiliating acceptance of democrats demands they will actually trust speaker nationalization of congressional politics has changed what it means to try to lead a congressional party what like to call leadership for speakers for presidents whatever often amounts to reading interests and preferences of different actors and figuring out how to bring them into a coalition to support policy that includes a little something for everyone pet projects and distribution of resources that what used to know as pork can help this process along but if legislators preferences are driven by national issues like defunding planned parenthood and opposing president then job of coalition building becomes that much more challenging boehner may not have been most talented legislative leader but his problems as greg indicates are also structural and systemic and to extent that they reflect infusion of national and often symbolic issues into congressional elections they may run deeper than tea partyestablishment split this post part of mischiefs of faction an independent political science blog featuring reflections party system see more mischiefs of faction posts here

corrections and clarifications an earlier version of this story misidentified alabama official leading efforts against samesex marriage in state it states chief justice washington when supreme court declared a constitutional right to samesex marriage last june man who won leading case warned that opponents would find new ways to push back will have to continue fight jim obergefell said then and he was right for nearly a year seesaw battles over religious exemptions and transgender rights have replaced what had been gay rights movements steady progress in winning protections against discrimination in states and cities legislative and legal skirmishes have been triggered by an intransigent alabama chief justice and a defiant kentucky county clerk a colorado baker and a washington state florist and most recently a conservative backlash that has traveled east from texas to mississippi to north carolina never thought this had to do with just marriage says kristen waggoner senior vice president of legal advocacy alliance defending freedom represents many gay rights opponents in court this about more than marriage its also about more than bathrooms although a north carolina law that denies transgender people right to use public restrooms corresponding to their gender identity has dominated lgbt rights debate for past two months across country gay rights movement has been met with local opposition sometimes where it was least expected such as houston had three times elected a lesbian mayor that has forced movement back defensive less than a year after its greatest success supreme courts decision in obergefell v hodges that extended samesex marriage nationwide to some this next front in war says david stacy government affairs director human rights campaign nations largest gay rights organization some of intensity other side some of emotional intensity comes from that both sides in battle say its about individual rights lgbt rights to live free from discrimination and religious or moral rights to live free from government interference it extends to education employment and housing and it may not be long before a lawsuit headed back to supreme court think this moment going to be shortlived but a lot of damage can be done in this moment says shannon minter legal director national center for lesbian rights it will ultimately be up to supreme court to decide whether title vii of civil rights act and title ix of education amendments act fully protect transgender people and for that matter gay people justice anthony kennedys historic ruling samesex marriage included a single paragraph warning of war to come first amendment ensures that religious organizations and persons are given proper protection as they seek to teach principles that are so fulfilling and so central to their lives and faiths and to their own deep aspirations to continue family structure they have long revered kennedy conceded same true of those who oppose samesex marriage for other reasons those battles had begun long before supreme courts decision in states where samesex marriage already was legal merchants who refused to participate in gay and lesbian weddings based religious objections were targets of lawsuits most of them lost early rounds and have appealed first major sign of resistance after high courts ruling came out of left field voters in houston where mayor annise parker in became one of first openly gay mayors of a major us city rejected a city councilpassed ordinance to protect residents based characteristics that included sexual orientation and gender identity simple slogan no men in womens bathrooms they identified an opening and exploited it says james esseks director of american civil liberties unions lgbt efforts then this year state legislatures in south dakota georgia mississippi and north carolina passed laws pushing back against lgbt rights bills were vetoed by republican governors dennis daugaard in south dakota and nathan deal in georgia but signed by mississippis phil bryant and north carolinas pat mccrory mississippis law protects those who deny services based religious objections to gay marriage or transgender people north carolinas prevents state and its municipalities from establishing civil rights protections based sexual orientation or gender identity youre seeing an explosion of religious liberty legislation in wake of obergefell says mat staver chairman of liberty counsel a conservative law firm and think tank involved in effort he calls it just tip of iceberg of what will see as obergefell begins to settle rest of country fight reached floor of house of representatives this month over an amendment intended to ban federal contractors from firing workers who are gay lesbian bisexual or transgender measure was blocked then passed then blocked again beset by such conflicts gay rights advocates have made little progress in past year their major postgay marriage agenda passing state laws protecting lgbt community against discrimination in employment housing and public accommodations utah was nd state to pass such a law in march since then gay rights activists have met a wall of opposition in remaining states mostly led by republican governors and legislatures people are using obergefell now to say can get married a sunday but then can go into work and get fired next day says travis weber director of center for religious liberty conservative family research council even so he says there s a need to protect religious dissenters two sets of legal skirmishes over religious exemptions and transgender rights have made it difficult for us to move forward esseks says he calls them responses to reality of freedom to marry nationwide there have been some victories for lgbt rights last month a federal appeals court in richmond ruled in favor of a transgender boys right to sue a virginia school district over its bathroom policy a handful of states led by new york have banned use of controversial conversion therapy minors it s not as though re not making any legislative progress are minter says even prospect of passing nondiscrimination laws in red states he says actually much less daunting after obergefell than it was before but energized by their loss supreme court last june religious objectors are fighting back amidst a presidential election that has divided country along geographical ideological and cultural lines what s stake right now so much bigger than whether someone has to shower with someone of opposite sex waggoner says think speakership one of toughest jobs in washington especially when it includes trying to contain a republican civil war two sunday attacks add to recent rise in fatal shootings of us police house majority leader kevin mccarthy r of california speaks during a new conference capitol hill in washington thursday after dropping out of race to replace house speaker john boehner shortly after republican rep kevin mccarthy stunned washington with another development in race for speaker of house frontrunner announced thursday that he was dropping out names of new candidates began circulating inevitably chatter quickly reached rep paul ryan r of wisconsin former vice presidential candidate chairman of powerful ways and means committee and budget hawk who many members believe person most capable of uniting deeply divided and turbulent house republican conference but chairman ryan promptly put down this second attempt to draft him as his texan gop colleague rep blake farenthold tweeted can blame him speakership one of toughest jobs in washington especially this speakership tasked with trying to end or contain a republican civil war in chamber it s going to take a lot of gumption and a lot of finesse and talent to bridge gap so yeah it s a difficult chore that very few people are willing to take rep harold rogers r of kentucky told a scrum of reporters thursday afternoon just hours earlier majority leader mccarthy an affable californian known for his listening skills and outreach capitulated in face of revolt from hardline house freedom caucus its or so members were set to vote against him in secret balloting his unscripted comments about political nature of benghazi special committee calling democratic presidential candidate hillary clinton to testify later this month also played a role in his decision he said mccarthy was attacked from all sides for that comment from democrats republicans and media when he was tagged as a future speaker who can t speak mccarthy was likely to survive hardliner defection thursday and win majority support from his gop conference needed to nominate him for speakership but it was not all clear that he could win votes needed to elect speaker house floor later this month standing before cameras in brightly lit marbled lobby of longworth house office building his wife beside him leader capitulated need a new face if were going to be strong gotta be percent united he said there s too much anger mccarthy told congressman rogers after taking himself out of running closeddoor balloting session according to roll call washington post reported members crying news who knows what ll happen people are crying they don t have any idea how this will unfold all rep peter king r of new york told post what most people see of speakership what shows up television but job carries awesome responsibility second in line to presidency manager of capitol complex presiding officer of house and that s easy part then there s role of chief messenger of party shaping agenda and trying to keep members in line every day to cross vote threshold number of votes it takes to pass a bill in member body and don t forget incessant fundraising takes up most weekends oh and negotiating with white house and senate then add hell no caucus of to rightwingers to this mix hardliners drove speaker john boehner r of ohio to announce his early retirement for oct in light of decision of his no to withdraw speaker boehner says he will stay until house votes to elect a new speaker mccarthy says he willing to stay as leader no date has been set for another leadership election some republicans want boehner to finish out his term as a congressman and speaker it s a very difficult job and having someone like john boehner there who has a lot of experience and has courage to do what s right a good thing says rep carlos curbelo r of florida congressman curbelo who describes speakership as more difficult than being president says he doesn t see any candidate from either party able to get votes that hasn t stopped new names from being floated even while other two candidates rep daniel webster of florida backed by freedom caucus and rep jason chaffetz of utah chairman of house oversight and government reform committee kept their hats in ring rightwingers were pleased to see mccarthy drop out they see him as boehner in a statement freedom caucus said it respects his decision to put conference ahead of himself and said next speaker needs to yield back power to membership republican caucus going through a period of transition says john feehery former spokesman to dennis hastert r of illinois longest serving republican speaker mccarthy represented a new generation of leaders in house where nearly half of members have served four years or less they re going to have to chart their own course and they re going to have to figure out how house works together and they re going to make mistakes he says that s one way to learn

as iowans prepare to cast first votes in presidential nominating process monday texas sen ted cruz and vermont sen bernie sanders hoped to defy polls and pull off upset victories in monday nights caucuses after months of campaigning and more than million spent advertising race for supremacy in iowa close in both parties among republicans latest polls show real estate billionaire donald trump holding a slim edge over cruz cruz who became first major candidate from either party to enter presidential race days ago has pinned his hopes to a sophisticated getoutthevote operation cruz has also modeled his campaign after past iowa winners visiting all of states counties and courting influential evangelical and conservative leaders if had told months ago that day before iowa caucuses wed be in a statistcal tie for first place would have been thrilled and exhilarated cruz told fox news late sunday republican caucus also first test of whether trump can turn legion of fans drawn to his plainspoken populism into voters scope of billionaires organization in iowa a mystery though trump himself has intensified his campaign schedule during final sprint including a pair of rallies monday dont have to win it trump said cbs face nation sunday im doing really well with evangelicals in iowa but im also doing tremendously well all over country with evangelicals think have a good chance of winning iowa by contrast cruz told fox news were not finding trumps troops ground they dont have an organization that perceptible cruz has also spent closing days of iowa campaign focused marco rubio trying to ensure florida senator doesnt inch into second place rubio viewed by many republicans as a more mainstream alternative to trump and cruz though hell need to stay competitive in iowa in order to maintain his viability democratic side sanders has rallied to close a point polling deficit against former secretary of state hillary clinton reviving memories of her disappointing showing in iowa eight years ago stick with clinton said as she rallied supporters sunday in council bluffs stick with a plan stick with experience sanders whose crowds have been large and generally younger than clintons urged voters to help him make history in a show of financial strength sanders campaign announced sunday it had raised million in january alone while sanders has a large team in iowa his operation got off to a later start particularly compared with clinton who has had staff ground in state for nearly a year think have a shot to win it if people come out sanders told abcs this week selfdescribed democratic socialists message that us economy rigged against middle class has appeared to resonate with an electorate that has grown frustrated with washington and given rise to insurgent candidates like sanders and trump campaigns were anxiously keeping an eye weather but a snowfall forecast to start monday night appeared more likely to hinder hopefuls in their rush out of iowa than any potential voters republican john kasich already had decamped to new hampshire sunday with new jersey gov chris christie and former florida gov jeb bush scheduled to follow monday afternoon hours before caucuses start trio of governors has had a light footprint in iowa banking instead strong showings in new hampshires feb primary to jumpstart their white house bids yet some republican leaders worry that if trump or cruz pull off a big victory in iowa it would be difficult to slow their momentum unlike in primaries where voters can cast their ballots throughout day caucuses begin across iowa pm cst democrats will gather locations and republicans nearly spots turnout was expected to be high iowa republican party expected gop turnout to top previous record of people in democrats also expect a strong turnout though not nearly as large as recordsetting people who caucused in contest between clinton barack obama and john edwards iowa has decidedly mixed results in picking parties eventual nominees past two republican caucus winners former arkansas gov mike huckabee and former pennsylvania sen rick santorum faded as race stretched but obamas unexpected victory was instrumental in his path to nomination easing anxieties of those who worried young black senator would struggle to win white voters while both parties caucus same night they do so with different rules republicans vote by private ballot states republican delegates are awarded proportionally based statewide vote democrats take a more interactive approach with voters forming groups and publicly declaring their support for a candidate if number of people in any group fewer than percent of total they can either choose not to participate or can join another viable candidates group those numbers are awarded proportionately based statewide and congressional district voting as iowa democrats determine their delegates to national convention fox news channels carl cameron and associated press contributed to this report

another deadline lapsed thursday in seemingly endless negotiations over iran s nuclear weapons programs from vienna secretary of state john kerry warned if tough decisions don t get made are absolutely prepared to call an end to this process that would be a mistake should instead keep negotiating without reaching a deal it s best outcome available now iran s inability to say yes to several very good deals it has been offered suggests negotiations may be a satisfactory end in itself for government of iran they have already achieved some sanctions relief they get stature of being secretary of state kerry s fulltime job their regional enemies are publicly feuding with washington and questioning commitment to their security it s not a bad outcome for ayatollah khameni especially if he fears domestic political repercussions of reintegrating with world agreement being haggled over in vienna by iran russia china britain france germany and united states will not eliminate iran s nuclear weapons program what under discussion how much nuclear infrastructure to let iran keep how extensive will be inspections of what remains and how much to give them for privilege inspections are key to any agreement focused reducing threat of iran s nuclear weapons programs technically agreement seeking to increase break out time how long it would take iran using known equipment and nuclear materials to construct a nuclear bomb these negotiations are seeking to increase iran s break out time from it s currently estimated months to a year it important to emphasize that dont honestly know how long it would take iran to make a nuclear weapon not for lack of effort by intelligence services but most of what do know about iranian nuclear weapons programs was revealed to us and there remains much do not know about past and current iranian programs should be deeply skeptical that know extent of iran s nuclear weapons work or have accounted for all its facilities congress has long been a force for good iran policy erecting sanctions for obama administration now takes credit senators corker and cardin have ensured that if there an agreement in vienna congress will now have days to review president s handiwork something administration had desperately tried to avoid secretary kerry published his understanding of framework for agreement concluded in april any further concessions will be carefully scrutinized many of arguments made by white house so far in defense of agreement will not hold up under careful questioning any agreement reached will ease sanctions iran and russia will surely never agree to reinstate them no matter how outrageous iranian cheating deal so sanctions will be unrecoverable since much of iranian economy organized to benefit revolutionary guards and citizen militia they will be primary beneficiaries of billion in revenue resulting from lifting just nuclear weapons related sanctions an iran deal would solve a political problem for president obama he committed to prevent iran acquiring a nuclear weapon yet so very clearly does not want to have to use military force against iran a nuclear deal solves his problem as a political matter but has already created security problems as regional allies hedge against his intentions a continuing negotiation not harried by deadlines might vitiate some of insecurity it prevents us lifting sanctions that will be impossible to put back once removed it provides exposure did not have to iranian leaders and perhaps intelligence their nuclear infrastructure it keeps us aligned with three european union states whose sanctions have been an essential constraint iranian government it makes more difficult russian and chinese support to iran and it leaves us free to conduct espionage of kind that has prevented iran from crossing nuclear threshold so far as know iran doesn t appear to have made fundamental choice to give up its nuclear weapons programs until it does continuing negotiations a better outcome than an agreement kori schake a hoover institution research fellow stanford university and a contributor to foreign policy s shadow government blog

parents are angry that show available cbbc website features a transgender storyline inappropriate for their children and concerned campaigners said it could sow seeds of confusion in young minds programme just a girl depicts an yearold s struggle to get hormones that stunt puberty making it easier to have sexchange surgery in future one mother writing mumsnet website said her daughter had become worried after seeing video she said her girl who likes wearing boys clothes and playing football had asked anxiously if that means she was a boy tory mp peter bone said it beggars belief that bbc making this programme freely available to children as young as six entirely share anger of parents who just want to let children be children it completely inappropriate for such material to be cbbc website and shall be writing to bbc bosses to demand they take it down as soon as possible former culture secretary maria miller voiced her concerns over bbc tackling subject in an ageappropriate way saying such issues should be raised where children can have support from parents and tory mp julian brazier said this programme very disappointing and inappropriate children are very impressionable and this going to confuse and worry them family campaigner norman wells said it irresponsible of bbc to introduce impressionable children as young as six to idea that they can choose to be something other than their biological sex just a girl fictional video diary of a child who calls herself amy and dresses as a girl it hosted cbbc website aimed children aged between six and in halfhour programme amy played by an actress reveals she was born a boy called ben but has already started using pubertyhalting drugs such hypothalamic blockers provoked a furore two years ago when mail sunday revealed an nhs clinic was willing to give them to children as young as nine critics cited research claiming that most teenagers confused about their gender never go through with surgery with many realising they are gay bbc row comes amid growing controversy over gender issues fuelled by a number of highprofile cases in one a christian couple were threatened with having their yearold daughter taken away because they oppose her plans to become a boy in another a sevenyearold boy was ordered to be removed from his mother s care as she was raising him as female causing him a great deal of emotional harm in just a girl amy says when was born mum said dad was so pleased that he had a boy to take to football but mum knew was different she realised early that was born in wrong body she adds mum supported when did a powerpoint presentation to class about transitioning and that wasn t going to come to school in boys clothes any more but girls clothes wasn t ben was amy later amy shown telling a friend josh a boy who wants to be recognised as a girl that she hormone blockers saying it took ages to get them after loads of tests and talks clinic once they realised was trans for real got them she says in another entry amy tells viewers she has developed a crush a boy called liam but confides liam thinks m just a girl but m not m trans and what s he going to say if he finds out stop being friend why m still aren t child psychotherapist dr dilys daws said programme could confuse children she said that while it was natural for youngsters to wonder what it would be like to be opposite sex bbc was irresponsible to feature extreme step of gender change for sixyearolds because they were too young to grapple with such issues programme generated hundreds of comments mumsnet one mother who said her sevenyearold had watched show asked am being unreasonable to think this an inappropriate topic for a young age group another replied don t think this remotely suitable for a sevenyearold to start suggesting that children can be transgender when they re far too young to actually have a gender reckless and damaging a small boy who told that he can become a girl may take this as meaning that sex changes are possible that sometime in future he ll wake up with a girl s body another user added don t think hormone therapy should be normalised any more than yearolds drinking or doing recreational drugs should be normalised other critics slammed bbc mr wells director of family education trust said more promote idea that a boy can be born into a girl s body and a girl can be born into a boy s body and that drugs and surgery can put things right more children will become utterly confused respecting and preserving a child s birth sex should be seen as a child protection issue but some parents mumsnet were more positive one wrote don t believe there too young for stuff like this earlier teach children that everyone different and that nobody normal better dr polly carmichael a clinical psychologist specialising in transgender children said raising awareness of these issues best way to challenge stigma and discrimination associated with identity issues programmes like just a girl can contribute to a healthy and informed public discussion bbc said just a girl about a fictional transgender character trying to make sense of world deal with bullying and work out how to keep her friends are universal themes that many children relate to and has had a positive response from audience cbbc aims to reflect true life providing content that mirrors lives of house speaker paul d ryan attempted to lift horizons of his party with a speech last week in he called for a competition of ideas rather than insults and constructive political debate rather than politics of demonization ryan s speech was aimed pulling republican party away from donald trump s embrace though he never actually mentioned trump by name events quickly showed what he up against speaker was quickly drowned out by a snarling argument between trump and sen ted cruz of texas over their wives that almost eclipsed terrorist attacks in brussels in us media by week s end republican race had gone into gutter over tabloid charges of infidelity senator vehemently denied and for he blamed new york billionaire who called it unfounded a race that seemed already bottom managed to find another low ryan s speech was a relatively highminded moment in middle of this mud fight of a republican nominating contest his effort to rescue party from a coming crisis laudable but root causes of condition go far beyond trump frontrunner for nomination of republican party as much a reflection of condition as a cause a reality that ryan rwis touched only lightly in calling for a more positive and uplifting approach to politics by all sides means stopping trump alone won t necessarily solve all of party s problems four years ago scholars thomas mann then with brookings institution and norman ornstein then and now with american enterprise institute published a book examining breakdown in american politics it was titled it s even worse than it looks authors took aim gridlocked and dysfunctional politics of washington and broader issue of political polarization that has become endemic in recent years they were unsparing but not evenhanded in their critique they were ahead of others in describing underlying causes of polarization as asymmetrical with republican party in particular its most hardline faction as deserving of far more of blame for breakdown in governing mann and ornstein are back again with a second and updated paperback edition called it s even worse than it was paperback arrives in middle of most raucous presidential campaign in memory one that has exposed even more fissures fractures and divisions within republican party coalition what played out primarily in party s congressional wing has come to consume presidential nominating contest in their own ways trump and cruz have brought to surface economic and cultural anger among many of those in party s base as well as distrust of party leadership same motivating forces behind freedom caucus rebels in house republican conference current campaign only adds fuel to mannornstein thesis of a republican party war with itself in ways that have helped cripple governing process trump and cruz reflect yearning within republican base for antiestablishment outsiders to topple insiders in washington ohio gov john kasich third remaining candidate for nomination a dissenting voice calling for cooperation and compromise this point he not just a dissenting voice he a minority voice in presidential competition unless he can start winning more primaries trump and ryan represent bookends in a political debate that has considerable consequences for republican party and for country trump s position as frontrunner not only highlights degree to party being taken over by antiestablishment forces but also foreshadows possibility of a significant defeat in november if as gop nominee trump unable to reverse his standing among women hispanics african americans and other voting groups ryan represents something far different politics grounded in ideas and policies and an attitude of goodwill toward opposition that he inherited from his mentor jack kemp former house member from buffalo who prodded his party to be more open and inclusive yet ryan s speech left unanswered key questions about his capacity to change behavior of his party s conference in house and in particular degree to he willing to find a governing coalition apart from hardliners in freedom caucus as country s highestranking republican elected official ryan symbolizes establishment s backlash to trump s candidacy a backlash that has so far failed to stop new york businessman s march to nomination resistance might yet succeed whether it does or doesn t it raises question of whether this presidential campaign ultimately will produce a true course change for party or merely end up intensifying forces that have brought it to this moment put that question to ornstein in an email exchange friday this really believe an existential crisis for republican party he wrote will it be a ryanstyle conservative problemsolving party or will it be either a trumpstyle authoritarian nativist and protectionist party or a cruzstyle radical antigovernment party content with blowing things up as they now stand or just as possible will party break apart with no clue as to what will replace it or how pieces will fit into broader political system prospects for a crackup are real given what trump s candidacy has revealed about party s fractured coalition trump s views issues outlined campaign trail and in a recent interview with washington post editorial board represent a fundamental break with many of conservative ideas that have been party s core for years trump s constituency finds his support for protecting rather than transforming social security and medicare appealing his words of praise for work of planned parenthood apart from performing abortions are anathema to many religious conservatives his views trade run counter to freetrade philosophy of gop elites his comments about reevaluating us role in nato shocked many in republican foreignpolicy establishment that s threat ryan and others in party see as they watch nominating contest move into next rounds of primaries but it isn t clear that what speaker advocated in his speech would be enough to put republican party in a better place even absent trump house republicans are still an unruly group and with some exceptions gop still prefers to try to do business with itself republican party remains a party of protest it continues to struggle to demonstrate that national level it can be a true governing party

rise of mandatory vaccinations means end of medical freedom source antimedia mandatory vaccinations are about to open up a new frontier for government control through war drugs bureaucrats arbitrarily dictate what people can and can t put into their bodies but that violation pales in comparison to forcibly medicating millions against their will voluntary and informed consent are essential in securing individual rights and without it selfownership will never be respected liberal stronghold of california trailblazing encroaching new practice and recently passed laws mandating that children and adults must have certain immunizations before being able to attend schools or work in certain professions longstanding religious and philosophical exemptions that protect freedom of choice have been systematically crushed by state california s senate bill went into effect july st and marked most rigid requirements ever instituted for vaccinations law forces students to endure a total of doses to complete federally recommended vaccines while allowing more to be added any time any family that doesn t go along will have their child barred from attending licensed day care facilities inhome daycares public or private schools and even after school programs over years california has developed a reputation for pushing vaccines their youth assembly bill was passed in and lowered age of consent for std prevention vaccines to just years old included in assortment of shots being administered was infamous gardasil just a few years later was center of a lawsuit that yielded victims a million settlement from us government paid out funds from national vaccine injury compensation program vaccinate all children act of an attempt to implement this new standard nationwide and although it has stalled in house it will likely be reintroduced next time country gripped by fear of a pandemic debate surrounding vaccinations commonly framed as a moral struggle between benefits to collective and selfish preferences of individual but since outbreak scares of zika measles and ebola rhetoric has taken a turn toward authoritarianism it s commonly stated by cdc and most mainstream doctors that unvaccinated are putting health of everyone else risk but truth isn t so black and white herd immunity theory has been consistently used to validate expansion of vaccine programs but it still doesn t justify removal of choice from individual classic exchange of freedom for perceived safety a no brainer for millions of americans who are willing to use government to strap their neighbors down and forcibly inject them for greater good anyone who expresses concern about possible side effects immediately branded as conspiratorial or antiscience yet controversial claims that certain vaccine variants cause neurological disorders like autism have led some people to swear off inoculations altogether this allornothing dynamic has completely polarized issue and prevents any reasonable discussion from taking place either accept all of cdc s recommended doses of vaccines between birth and age or want to bring back measles polio and probably black plague other extreme side of debate if fail to acknowledge all vaccines as dangerous re an ignorant sheep through internet disinformation has become widespread and created a movement of people that have written off all benefits accomplished through immunizations these individuals are unable or unwilling to separate science from shady institutions that develop and distribute new vaccines even if thimerosal and mercury based preservatives cause adverse reactions in some patients it doesn t detract from advantages vaccine technology provides in this debate like most others in us both sides are swept up in emotion and ignorance regardless public s trust in vaccinations has been eroded by reputations of those companies producing them pharmaceutical giants like merck and pfizer make billions from distribution of these shots and potential profits after a mandate are enough to corrupt morals of almost anyone in one example former cdc director dr julie gerberding left her post government agency in to work in merck s vaccine division an investigative report published by british medical journal last year found cdc downplays its ties to pharmaceutical industry further by buying support of politicians like hillary clinton who received more donations from pharmaceutical companies and their employees than any other candidate this year these huge companies are able to expand their influence in directing government policy maintaining control over what put into own bodies a fundamental right but for now standing up to these government decrees only means ostracism from education system and criticism from peers in future however punishments for disobedience will likely only grow stricter an orange county doctor named bob sears already in crosshairs of california s medical board after excusing a twoyearold from future vaccinations mother expressed concern that her daughter had an adverse reaction to a previous shot describing child as becoming limp like a ragdoll for hours after last dose dr sears alternative treatment recommendations break from rules dictated by sb and now his reputation as well as his career are in jeopardy this new authority to strip doctors of their medical licenses for simply going against stateimposed standards opens door for persecution of medical professionals who resist any government regulation a vaccination an invasive medical procedure that can have different effects each and every individual nuremberg code s first principle voluntary consent but it seems lessons of history have been completely forgotten by today s leaders transition of these shots from recommended to required well underway and those who think ends justify means are willing to forcibly make sure everyone else complies new benchmark set by california symbolizes a precedent that could be mimicked across nation without having discretion to choose medications are injected into body or child s how can anyone convince themselves they are free this overreach and collusion can often be dismissed as a trivial issue but fact that voluntary consent under attack speaks volumes to extent that state power has metastasized

this underwater lake actually helping scientists deal with a much bigger mystery credit evnautilus it may seem impossible but scientists have discovered what can only be deemed as a lake under sea dubbed jacuzzi of despair brinefilled pool deadly for majority of creatures that dare enter it but proving to be highly informational for those that are studying it located in gulf of mexico lake rises about feet off of ocean floor and four to five times saltier than surrounding ocean it s also twice as warm rich with methane what makes it bubble like a jacuzzi and dense with hydrogen sulfide this makes it incompatible with sea water and a completely separate entity dr erik cordes associate professor of biology temple university in philadelphia who discovered pool with several colleagues told seeker it was one of most amazing things in deep sea go down into bottom of ocean and are looking a lake or a river flowing it feels like are not this world since conditions of pool are so foreign to humans and most of creatures living under sea it s allowing scientists to get a glimpse of what life might be like in extreme circumstances what started as an interesting lake in middle of ocean has now become heart of experimentation for one of most mysterious subjects known to man space dr cordes said there s a lot of people looking these extreme habitats earth as models for what might discover when go to other planetsthe technology development in deep sea definitely going to be applied to worlds beyond own what makes pool even more unique that it has a lively ecosystem that has evolved throughout centuries to include more species while larger animals such as deepsea crabs die as soon as they enter pool other creatures have adapted to atmosphere of water and even thrive it giant mussels formed symbiotic bacteria in their gills to feed off of hydrogen sulfide and methane gas from pool and specially adapted shrimp and tube worms were able to survive harsh conditions any other animals that entered were immediately killed pickled by salt and preserved forever watch video below to see beautiful lake for yourself and creatures who did and did not survive conditions supreme court it would seem did not want to see what it was up to wednesday robed nine were hearing oral arguments in king v burwell a legal effort by conservatives to dismantle obamacare and probably most politically charged case to appear before high court since bush v gore but as always there was no video of proceedings and curiously court chose not to release sameday audio of argument as it did in bush v gore and has done in other highprofile cases since then went to argument as have for last decade to attempt to paint for readers a verbal picture of atmospherics in room such as samuel alito s eye rolls sonia sotomayor s hectoring and clarence thomas s states of repose but this time court staff placed in back corner three feet from door blocking view of justices were two redvelvet curtains a marble pillar another marble pillar and a closed brass gate carved with images of acorns oak leaves dolphins helmets and plumes animal heads and ten commandments ultimately though there will be no hiding what happened in that chamber wednesday morning ninety minutes of lopsided argument in favor of obama administration s defense cast significant doubt what had been a plausible challenge to obamacare s legality conservative majority could still knock down law of course but given ambiguity exposed wednesday it would now be a breathtaking surprise for justices to cause such massive upheaval taking healthcare immediately from million and causing a death spiral for rest of obamacare based such a slender legal reed four liberal justices furiously picked apart arguments of michael carvin who had also argued unsuccessfully in a challenge to healthcare law alito and antonin scalia were not as aggressive as usual in their questioning of obama administration s lawyer and chief justice john roberts was almost as silent as thomas anthony kennedy perpetual swing vote had some serious doubts about argument against obamacare kennedy said that while perhaps will prevail in plain words of statute there s a serious constitutional problem if adopt argument along with a violation of federalism because states would be coerced to embrace obamacare exchanges or enter an insurance death spiral he said sometimes think of things government doesn t kennedy informed him issue whether language in affordable care act calling for each state to establish a healthcare exchange means that people are not eligible for subsidies in states where federal government created exchanges because state refused to do so take away those subsidies and obamacare collapses one part of law favors critics interpretation but other parts of law and broader context of law contradict such an interpretation given this talmudic dispute over text it s almost inconceivable that justices supposed practitioners of judicial modesty would consider that justification sufficient to tear apart nation s social fabric opponents of law went through great contortions to bring this case they re arguing against tax credits and thus in favor of tax increases they had trouble finding people who had sufficient injury to have standing in case and in wednesday s arguments it became apparent that only one of four plaintiffs clearly qualified more difficulty came when carvin argued that states would still get benefit of exchanges even without subsidies justice elena kagan pointed out that he argued opposite in his last appearance whichever legal argument law s opponents were relying moment it was secondary to political argument against obamacare scalia in his attempt to justify social upheaval that would come if court jettisoned law asked government s top lawyer donald verrilli really think congress just going to sit there while all of these disastrous consequences ensue congress adjusts enacts a statute that takes care of problem it happens all time there was laughter in courtroom after argument hundreds of activists whipped up by some members of that very congress waved signs and traded taunts as if they were rival fans a basketball game stand up fight back health care under attack chanted one side liberty follow law other side shouted back in unlikely event roberts court uses an ambiguous textual dispute to overturn most significant social legislation of era there will be no place to hide from national conflagration that follows read more from dana milbank s archive follow him twitter or subscribe to his updates facebook

us republican presidential candidate donald trump delivers a campaign speech about national security in manchester new hampshire us june donald trump says his presidential campaign revoking washington post s press credentials and post isn t first major news outlet to be barred from trump s rallies and events trump previously banned politico buzzfeed news huffington post and others from his campaign events according to new york times trump called washington post phony and dishonest in a facebook post where he explained that he decided to pull press credentials due to paper s incredibly inaccurate coverage and reporting of his campaign trump took issue with a headline post wrote monday that read donald trump suggests president obama was involved with orlando shooting headline was changed monday afternoon to read donald trump seems to connect president obama to orlando shooting post story covered trump s statements during a fox news interview monday when asked to explain why he called for obama to resign after shooting he answered in part he doesnt get it or he gets it better than anybody understands its one or other and either one unacceptable washington post spokeswoman kristine coratti kelly told associated press in an email that post changed headline shortly after posting story to more properly reflect what trump said did so own trump campaign never contacted us about it kelly said washington post editor martin baron said in a statement tuesday that decision to revoke paper s press credentials nothing less than a repudiation of role of a free and independent press baron defended post s coverage of trump s campaign so far and vowed to continue with honest honorable and accurate reporting trump has said that if he were to become president news organizations that have criticized him will have problems he threatened to sue washington post in january over an article about bankruptcy of his atlantic city casino this would be a very difficult suit to win thanks to first amendments historically strong protection of journalists but trump has said as president he hopes to weaken libel law that protects journalists covering public figures under american libel law supreme court has repeatedly endorsed under first amendment it difficult for public figures to sue reporters who criticize them because they must demonstrate that factually inaccurate statements were made with actual malice or reckless disregard for truth legal experts doubt trump as president could change libel law through executive order change would likely require a constitutional amendment nonetheless denying reporters access to his campaign events within trump s current power chris cillizza wrote for washington post tuesday that politicians and journalists inevitably have a tense relationship job of journalists post and everywhere else to give voters fullest and most accurate picture of two people who want to represent all of us as president that a task that inherently conflict least times with story candidates want to tell about themselves that tension natural and often leads to uncomfortable relationships between candidates and media who cover them this report it seems increasingly likely that jeb bush will run for president even his son talking up prospects what remains to be seen whether jeb bush can actually win a lot of gop establishment types are excited about a bush candidacy but dont expect grassroots to eagerly embrace a former florida governor who has made a habit of moderately breaking from conservative orthodoxy big issues like immigration or who recently mildly singled out fox news for criticism as editor of washington free beacon said its not hard to understand huntsman comparison former utah governor among most conservative in country during his tenure was tarred as a basebetraying moderate before his campaign even officially began to say he flamed out would be inaccurate in that he never lit up in first place huntsmans muchhyped campaign was basically over as soon as it began jeb already has baggage of his brother to contend with last thing he needs to invite are comparisons to illfated huntsman campaign so how can he avoid this fate here are four pieces of unsolicited advice toughen up there are basically two ways that voters judge candidates one issues and candidates record them other based temperament rather subjective sense of who this person for conservative voters who might be uneasy about bush his stance some issues problematic support for things like common core and immigration reform arent exactly red meat for gop base but bush can balance this by being tough huntsman was seen as ideologically moderate and temperamentally weak thats a horrible combination ted cruz seen as ideologically stern and temperamentally tough a better combination as far as base concerned but probably one that catches up to him with general electorate who has a good combination chris christies tough persona covers a multitude of sins issues and indeed allows him to be a bit more ideologically moderate another example john mccain moderate some issues including immigration reform his toughguy image helped provide balance enough to win nomination least base voters both sides of aisle tend to conflate toughness with ideological purity im probably more conservative than ann coulter who backed chris christie for president in and defended romneycare but im willing to bet everyone would suspect that coulter who never afraid to take knives out way more conservative than style sometimes trumps substance most candidates have a natural proclivity to be hard or soft mike huckabee might be best modern politician playing nice and tough but even here latter often comes across as inauthentic and ironically as mean problem for jeb that he seems to be temperamentally moderate as well as ideologically moderate he needs to overcome this by getting tough this most important thing he can do dont flipflop being tough sometimes mean sticking to guns and standing up to base see bill clintons sister souljah moment now might think that smart move for jeb would instead be to pander to base and change his positions this almost always a terrible idea and only compounds a candidates image problem conservative base now not only distrusts ideologically but also sees as a wimp essentially same as some caricature of an effete liberal yes there may be ways to massage these things to stress some issues and downplay others but answer for jeb isnt to shed his moderate positions that will only compound his problems he cannot change his positions no matter how tempting it may be doing so will make him look weak and remember what said about being tough dont try to change gop base this one probably seems obvious but jeb bush isnt some backbencher running to get a message out or pull other candidates toward him certain issues as elizabeth warren might if she challenged hillary clinton if he runs it should be to win that means his purpose isnt to lecture base or shame base or change base its to win election if he wants to win an argument instead of an election he should stay home and write blog posts like people can tell if dont like or respect them this true of almost every profession in jerry maguire title characters sports agent mentor tells him unless love everybody cant sell anybody same true in writing world eb white said no one can write decently who distrustful of readers intelligence if jeb harbors any resentment toward republican base he either needs to sit this one out because it will show or overcome it by flipping a psychological switch and reminding himself constantly that these are good people and that he loves them dont lamely suck up to gop base either realize this might seem contradictory but its not and while jeb cannot and should not be in business of overtly pandering doing so would make him look inauthentic and weak he absolutely must reach out to conservative opinion leaders he must be accessible and this will be a problem for him because he a bit cloistered and because of starpower associated with being a bush he can already seem intimidating and likely already has a team of handlers charged with isolating him from masses rudy giuliani suffered from this as well how to overcome this best model ive witnessed was mccain who in was incredibly accessible to conservative writers in that regard he probably overperformed jeb should read this jeb bush doesnt have to be jon huntsman in fact he might well be next president of united states but to win hell have to toughen up stay true to himself and walk a tricky path between alienating and bowing before gop base it wont be easy but he can do it

lt steve gozzo usn via wikimedia commons london even height of day skies in many parts of northern iraq are dark as isis torches oil wells and oilfilled defensive trenches in its retreat artillery fire and bombing raids by us aircraft and others battling isis are also causing conflagrations oil installations aid teams near town of qayyarah about kilometres south of isis stronghold of mosul talk of escaping civilians being covered in oil residues everywhere covered in a fine dusting of black soot and grime one aid worker from save children charity told bbc and children met were covered in it their hands were black their feet were black and their hair was matted they were coming out in rashes developing problems with their lungs deliberate pollution there are fears that as isis comes under ever greater pressure it will unleash scorched earth tactics setting alight ever more oil wells and deliberately polluting waters of tigris and euphrates two of region s main rivers supply water and power to millions setting oil fields alight could also have wider climaterelated consequences during saddam hussein s invasion and subsequent retreat from kuwait in iraqis set alight nearly kuwaiti oil wells one stage in march it was calculated that up to six million barrels of oil were being burned each day result was daytime darkness and long plumes of black smoke across a wide area of gulf though longterm impact of kuwait s oil fires climate still being assessed release of vast amounts of climatechanging greenhouse gases into atmosphere considered to have added to problems of warming both a regional and a global scale gulf region one of fastestwarming in world with many areas forecast to be uninhabitable in not too distant future because of higher temperatures and chronic water shortages longterm damage pollution from kuwait oil fires and ruptured oil pipelines are also believed to have caused serious longterm damage to waters of gulf there are fears that with pressure group mounting similar developments could unfold in northern iraq as isis torches oil installations years later in recent years iraq has been trying to ramp up its oil production in order to raise more revenues partly to fund war against isis but much of development of iraq s fossil fuel resources has been badly planned and mismanaged world bank says that globally approximately billion cubic metres of natural gas produced together with oil are burned or flared off each year adding million tonnes of carbon dioxide emissions to atmosphere from exploding fuel barrels to exposure to carcinogenic chemicals and inhalational toxins these makeshift oil refineries will have a longlasting health impact iraq now one of world s leading gasflaring countries a lack of pipelines and infrastructure means that its gas mostly burned off meanwhile country has been forced to import large amounts of gas from neighbouring iran in order to meet its energy needs across northern iraq and eastern syria country s main oilproducing region isis controls large numbers of oil wells and derives considerable income from selling fossil fuels black market a lack of maintenance and expertise means that many of these installations are a hazard to environment badlyrun oil facilities also cause considerable human suffering a recent report by pax a netherlandsbased church grouping says that more than makeshift oil refineries are operating in isiscontrolled deir ezzor area of syria thousands of civilians many of them children are forced to work these crude basicallyrun facilities from exploding fuel barrels to exposure to carcinogenic chemicals and inhalational toxins these makeshift oil refineries will have a longlasting health impact communities and their environment says report kieran cooke a founding editor of climate news network a former foreign correspondent for bbc and financial times he now focuses environmental issues advertisement

new yorkers fight to overturn ballot selfie ban new yorkers fight to overturn ballot selfie ban by new york voters are suing state arguing it unconstitutional to ban them from showing their completed ballots to others via social media ballot selfie ban becoming a hot topic this election affecting even celebrities like justin timberlake three new yorkers eve silber rebecca white and michael emperor filed federal lawsuit in wednesday seeking a judge to declare election law banning ballot selfies unconstitutional according to attorney representing group taking a photograph of a filled out ballot a powerful political statement that demonstrates importance of voting without photograph message loses its power says lawsuit filed by lawyer leo glickman in manhattan federal court according to new york daily news under current state law showing a marked ballot to another voter considered a misdemeanor can result in prison time and a hefty fine according to court papers glickman seeking a court injunction to stop officials from enforcing law before november election similar laws against ballot selfies have been struck down in michigan indiana and new hamsphire as a violation of first amendment guarantee of freedom of speech selfies are being allowed polls in connecticut but officials will be watching for whether practice becomes disruptive for voters in new jersey assemblyman raj mukherji pushing for a bill that would protect voters right to take selfies ballot box cbs radio reported singeractor justin timberlake got into some trouble this week after posting a photo of himself voting in tennessee his instagram page picture prompted a reminder of law from state officials that such photography against law timberlake addressed controversy during an appearance tonight show wednesday telling host jimmy fallon he thought he was inspiring people with picture and had no idea it was illegal taking photos inside a voting booth illegal in tennessee under a state law as it in other states according to a review of laws banning ballot selfies conducted by associated press via rt this piece was reprinted by rinf alternative news with permission or license

facebook must have thought online news game was pretty easy two years ago it plucked a small team of about a dozen bright hungry twentysomethings fresh out of journalism school or entrylevel reporting jobs it stuck them in a basement paid them contractor wages and put them to work selecting and briefly summarizing day s top news stories and linking to news sites that covered them it called them curators not reporters their work appeared in trending section of facebook home page and mobile app where it helped to define day s news for millions of facebook users tuesday facebook became subject of a senate inquiry over claims of anticonservative bias in its trending section senate commerce committee chairman john thune a south dakota republican sent mark zuckerberg a letter asking a series of pointed questions about how facebook chooses stories for section how it trains its curators who s responsible for their decisions and what steps it s taking to investigate bias claims he also asks for detailed records of stories that company decided not to include in trending section despite their popularity among facebook users inquiry followed a report by gizmodo s michael nunez monday in anonymous former facebook curators described subjective process by they assembled trending section facebook had publicly portrayed section can find near top right of facebookcom or under search tab facebook app as an algorithmically driven reflection of most popular stories its users are reading any given time but excurators said they often filtered out stories that were deemed questionable and added others they deemed worthy one a selfidentified conservative complained that this led to subtle yet pervasive liberal bias since most of curators were politically liberal themselves popular stories from conservative sites such as breitbart for instance were allegedly omitted unless more mainstream publications such as new york times also picked them up none of this should come as a surprise to any thoughtful person who has worked as a journalist humans are biased objectivity a myth or best an ideal that can be loosely approached through very careful practice of trained professionals news simply not neutral neither curation for that matter in either journalistic or artistic application of term there are ways to grapple with this problem honestly to attempt to identify and correct for one s biases to scrupulously disclose them to employ an ideologically diverse staff perhaps even to reject objectivity as an ideal and embrace subjectivity but can t begin to address subjective nature of news without first acknowledging it and facebook has gone out of its way to avoid doing that for reasons that are central to its identity as a technology company for that matter don t get that big by admitting that re a media company as new york times john herrman and mike isaac point out percent of americans surveyed by pew view news media as a negative influence country for technology companies that number just percent it s very much in facebook s interest to remain a social network in public s eyes even in face of mounting evidence that it s something much bigger than that and it s in facebook s interest to shift responsibility for controversial decisions from humans whom know to be biased to algorithms tend to lionize human values shape trending section too algorithm that surfaces stories might skirt questions of bias by simply ranking them in order of popularity thus delegating responsibility for story selection from facebook s employees to its users even that notion that what s popular worth highlighting represents a human value judgment albeit one that s not particularly vulnerable to accusations of political bias that s why twitter isn t in same hot water over its own simpler trending topics module problem with an algorithm that simply harnesses wisdom of crowd that crowd isn t always wise most popular stories any given time might well be misleading or sensationalist or even full of lies that s why facebook felt need to hire humans to oversee it this in keeping with company s broader push for what it calls quality content another term that entails value judgments without copping to them but facebook instead opted to hire cheap contractors and went to claim that their role simply to confirm that topics are in fact trending news in real world and not for example similarsounding topics or misnomers that s a dubious claim even if think allegations of liberal bias are trumped up if curators job was really just about cleaning up data facebook seems to have forgotten to tell that to curators themselves who described their mandate very differently to gizmodo they said they were encouraged to prioritize stories from certain outlets deemed reputable to avoid news about among other topics facebook itself and to replace word twitter in headlines with something more vague like social media that may not be political bias but it s bias all same they also described choosing stories for trending section that may not have been surfaced by algorithm but that seemed to them to be important or worthwhile like stories about conflict in syria or black lives matter movement problem will not be solved by firing bad apples or instituting tougher guidelines only way for facebook to extricate itself from this mess to admit that journalism isn t as simple as it thought it s to stop treating curators like drones and stop treating news like a data set to be optimized it s to build a real human curation team with a real editor in charge and an ethos and a mission and an understanding of responsibilities involved in shaping how news framed to billion people surely a company that pays its interns a month in salary and benefits can afford it

a focus in us presidential race charges of a rigged economy indeed within many countries inequality has risen but not everywhere worldwide in fact inequality actually going down humanity it seems not leaving its poorest behind this conclusion comes from work of an eminent expert inequality branko milanovic he spent decades studying data world bank and now works city university in new york in a new book global inequality a new approach for age of globalization he makes a case that rapid growth of poorer countries since has brought first decline in inequality since industrial revolution mr milanovic pessimistic about us reducing its inequality soon but he finds many proequality trends will continue to grow world s new middle class one trend what he calls propoor innovation such as ability of african farmers to use cellphones to check farm prices another use of online courses to educate poor people in skills sought by global companies china and other asian countries have led way in forging development policies that have helped close global income gap best inequalitybuster economic growth milanovic says but other efforts are needed such as equality in opportunities in brazil for example inequality has gone down because of better education milanovic s findings are reinforced by new research from tomáš hellebrandt and paolo mauro of peterson institute for international economics they find global inequality fell between and and they project number of people in poverty will fall from percent of total population to percent by ability to participate in and benefit from economic growth has immediate and tangible impacts lives of bulk of world s population two economists conclude very rich or very corrupt may still hide their wealth in tax havens politicians in developed countries may decry rising inequality but global trends and new data tell an alternative story about progress already made to lift poor
